title: Results summary
text: This page compiles the results of all the performed contrasts: in lysogeny, intra-strain time-course and inter-strain time-course.

title: Results summary
text:
Barplots
This plot shows the number of DE genes, both upregulated and downregulated, in each contrast. As we can see, the GIL01 vs GBJ002 contrast has the fewest DE genes, while the GIL16 vs GBJ002 contrast has the most.


Code
lysogeny_contrasts_size <- lysogeny_contrasts %>%
  select(Contrast, DE, Size) %>%
  mutate(
    Contrast = factor(Contrast, levels = levels(lysogeny_contrasts$Contrast)),
    DE = factor(DE, levels = levels(lysogeny_contrasts$DE))
  )

ggplot(lysogeny_contrasts_size, aes(x = Contrast, y = Size, fill = DE)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = Size),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    vjust = -0.4,
    size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("UP" = "red", "DOWN" = "blue")) +
  ylim(0, 600) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(x = "Contrast", y = "DE genes", fill = "DE")









This plot shows the number of enrichments found in each category. Most enrichments are found in the MF and CC categories of GO, while the fewest are found in the EC numbers. Again, the GIL01 vs GBJ002 contrast has the fewest enrichments, while the GIL16 vs GBJ002 contrast has the most.


Code
lysogeny_contrasts_enrichments <- lysogeny_contrasts %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !c(Group, Contrast, DE),
    names_to = "Category",
    values_to = "Count"
  ) %>%
  filter(Category != "Size") %>%
  mutate(
    Contrast = factor(Contrast, levels = levels(lysogeny_contrasts$Contrast)),
    DE = factor(DE, levels = levels(lysogeny_contrasts$DE)),
    Category = factor(Category, levels = colnames(lysogeny_contrasts)[4:ncol(lysogeny_contrasts)])
  )

ggplot(lysogeny_contrasts_enrichments, aes(x = Category, y = Count, fill = DE)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(Count == 0, NA, Count)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    vjust = -0.4, size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("UP" = "red", "DOWN" = "blue")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("COG", "KEGG", "GO (BP)", "GO (MF)", "GO (CC)", "EC (1)", "EC (2)", "EC (3)")) +
  facet_wrap(~ Contrast, nrow = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 40), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  labs(x = "Enrichment category", y = "Enrichments", fill = "DE")

Code
ggplot() +
  geom_hline(
    data = subset(line_data, DE == "UP"),
    aes(yintercept = as.numeric(COG) + 0.1),
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.75,
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    data = subset(line_data, DE == "DOWN"),
    aes(yintercept = as.numeric(COG) - 0.1),
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "dashed",
    linewidth = 0.75,
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = subset(line_data, DE == "UP"),
    aes(x = 0.35, y = as.numeric(COG) + 0.1, label = "UP"),
    colour = "red",
    vjust = -0.25,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3,
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = subset(line_data, DE == "DOWN"),
    aes(x = 0.35, y = as.numeric(COG) - 0.1, label = "DOWN"),
    colour = "blue",
    vjust = -0.25,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3,
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = lysogeny_cog,
    aes(
      x = Ratio,
      y = y_pos,
      size = Number,
      fill = minus_log10_p,
      shape = Group
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 0.35),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq_along(levels(lysogeny_cog$COG)),
    labels = levels(lysogeny_cog$COG)
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(limits = c(0, 20)) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    limits = c(1, 100),
    breaks = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100)
  ) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(
      "GIL01 vs GBJ002" = 21,
      "GIL16 vs GBJ002" = 22,
      "GIL16 vs GIL01" = 23
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Gene ratio",
    y = "COG category",
    size = "DE genes",
    fill = expression(paste("Adjusted p value (", -log[10], ")")),
    shape = "Contrast"
  ) +
  guides(
    fill  = guide_colorbar(order = 1),
    size  = guide_legend(order = 2),
    shape = guide_legend(order = 3)
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank()
  )

title: Results summary
text:
DE genes


Code
lysogeny_de_genes <- create_de_genes_table(group = "strains", filter = "Lysogeny")

colnames(lysogeny_de_genes) <- c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "GIL01 vs GBJ002", "GIL16 vs GBJ002", "GIL16 vs GIL01")

max_abs <- ceiling(max(abs(lysogeny_de_genes[, 4:ncol(lysogeny_de_genes)]), na.rm = TRUE))
brks <- seq(-max_abs, max_abs, length.out = 50)
clrs <- colorRampPalette(c("#5A8AC6", "white", "#F8696B"))(length(brks) + 1)

sketch <- withTags(table(
  class = "display",
  thead(
    tr(
      th(colspan = 3, style = "text-align: center; font-weight: bold;", "Gene annotation"),
      th(colspan = 3, style = "text-align: center; font-weight: bold;", "Lysogeny contrasts")
    ),
    tr(
      lapply(colnames(lysogeny_de_genes),
             function(x) th(style = "text-align: center;", x))
    )
  )
))

datatable(lysogeny_de_genes, container = sketch, rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c(4:6), digits = 2) %>%
  formatStyle(columns = c(4:6), backgroundColor = styleInterval(brks, clrs)) %>%
  formatStyle(columns = c(1, 2, 4:ncol(lysogeny_de_genes)), `text-align` = "center")

title: Results summary
text:
Enrichments summary
This table contains a summary of the enrichments for each category and contrast. The subcategory was used for KEGG and the description for GO. Although you can scroll, I recommend exporting using the Excel button and viewing the table in full screen.


Code
datatable(lysogeny_enrichments, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Group", "Contrast", "DE genes", "DE number", "COG", "KEGG", "GO (BP)", "GO (MF)", "GO (CC)", "EC (1)", "EC (2)", "EC (3)"),
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            autoWidth = TRUE,
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 3),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE))

title: Results summary
text:
Barplots
This plot shows the number of DE genes, both upregulated and downregulated, in each contrast per strain. As we can see, there doesn’t appear to be a difference in the number of DE genes according to the strain. This is because three independent models (i.e. three DESeq2 objects) were constructed for each set of contrasts, reflecting that the contrasts with fewer DE genes are those from early time points (0 and 10 minutes), while those performed with late time points (60 or 60-mock) show a large number of DE genes.


Code
intra_strain_time_course_contrasts_size <- intra_strain_time_course_contrasts %>%
  select(Group, Contrast, DE, Size) %>%
  mutate(
    Group = factor(Group, levels = levels(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts$Group)),
    Contrast = factor(Contrast, levels = levels(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts$Contrast)),
    DE = factor(DE, levels = levels(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts$DE))
  )

ggplot(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts_size, aes(x = Contrast, y = Size, fill = DE)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
           width = 0.75, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = Size),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    vjust = -0.4,
    size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("UP" = "red", "DOWN" = "blue")) +
  facet_wrap(~ Group, ncol = 1) +
  ylim(0, 800) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  labs(x = "Contrast", y = "DE genes", fill = "DE")









This plot shows the number of enrichments found in each category. Most enrichments are found in the BP GO category, unlike what happened in lysogeny, which were mainly found in MF and CC. In this plot, the contrasts between GBJ002 times show less enrichments than with GIL01 or GIL16, which would reflect the differences found in gene expression throughout MMC induction (even without induction) when any of these tectiviruses are present.


Code
intra_strain_time_course_contrasts_enrichments <- intra_strain_time_course_contrasts %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !c(Group, Contrast, DE, Size),
    names_to = "Category",
    values_to = "Count"
  ) %>%
  filter(Category != "Size") %>%
  mutate(
    Group = factor(Group, levels = levels(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts$Group)),
    Contrast = factor(Contrast, levels = levels(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts$Contrast)),
    DE = factor(DE, levels = levels(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts$DE)),
    Category = factor(Category, levels = colnames(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts)[4:ncol(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts)][colnames(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts)[4:ncol(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts)] != "Size"])
  )

ggplot(intra_strain_time_course_contrasts_enrichments, aes(x = Category, y = Count, fill = DE)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(Count == 0, NA, Count)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    vjust = -0.4, size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("UP" = "red", "DOWN" = "blue")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("COG", "KEGG", "GO (BP)", "GO (MF)", "GO (CC)", "EC (1)", "EC (2)", "EC (3)")) +
  facet_grid(Group ~ Contrast) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 70), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Enrichment category", y = "Enrichments", fill = "DE")

text:
DE genes


Code
intra_de_genes <- create_de_genes_table(group = "time_points")

colnames(intra_de_genes)[1:2] <- c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol")

max_abs <- ceiling(max(abs(intra_de_genes[, 4:ncol(intra_de_genes)]), na.rm = TRUE))
brks <- seq(-max_abs, max_abs, length.out = 50)
clrs <- colorRampPalette(c("#5A8AC6", "white", "#F8696B"))(length(brks) + 1)

lfc_cols <- colnames(intra_de_genes)[4:ncol(intra_de_genes)]
groups <- sub(" - .*", "", lfc_cols)
group_levels <- c("GBJ002", "GIL01", "GIL16")
group_counts <- sapply(group_levels, function(g) sum(groups == g))
second_row <- c(colnames(intra_de_genes)[1:3], sub(".* - ", "", lfc_cols))

sketch <- withTags(table(
  class = "display",
  thead(
    tr(
      th(colspan = 3, style = "text-align: center; font-weight: bold;", "Gene annotation"),
      lapply(seq_along(group_levels), function(i)
        th(colspan = group_counts[i], style = "text-align: center; font-weight: bold;", group_levels[i])
      )
    ),
    tr(
      lapply(second_row, function(x) th(style = "text-align: center;", x))
    )
  )
))

datatable(intra_de_genes, container = sketch, rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = 4:ncol(intra_de_genes), digits = 2) %>%
  formatStyle(columns = 4:ncol(intra_de_genes), backgroundColor = styleInterval(brks, clrs)) %>%
  formatStyle(columns = c(1, 2, 4:ncol(intra_de_genes)), `text-align` = "center")

title: Results summary
text:
Barplots
This plot shows the number of DE genes, both upregulated and downregulated, in each time per strains contrasts. As we can see, the highest number of DE genes are obtained at late time points, and especially in the contrasts of GBJ002 vs GIL01 and GIL16. This is because a combined model was used for all contrasts (i.e. a single DESeq2 object), reflecting that, considering both time and strain, the greatest transcriptional changes are found at late time points.


Code
inter_strain_time_course_contrasts_size <- inter_strain_time_course_contrasts %>%
  select(Group, Contrast, DE, Size) %>%
  mutate(
    Group = factor(Group, levels = levels(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts$Group)),
    Contrast = factor(Contrast, levels = levels(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts$Contrast)),
    DE = factor(DE, levels = levels(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts$DE))
  )

ggplot(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts_size, aes(x = Group, y = Size, fill = DE)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
           width = 0.75, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = Size),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    vjust = -0.4,
    size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("UP" = "red", "DOWN" = "blue")) +
  facet_wrap(~ Contrast, ncol = 1) +
  ylim(0, 700) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  labs(x = "Time points", y = "DE genes", fill = "DE")









This plot shows the number of enrichments found in each category. Most enrichments, as in the previous plot, are found at late times in the contrasts of GBJ002 vs GIL01 and GIL16. In contrast, the differences between both strains with tectivirus, although they exist, are minimal and are only found at the 30 and 60-mock times.


Code
inter_strain_time_course_contrasts_enrichments <- inter_strain_time_course_contrasts %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !c(Group, Contrast, DE, Size),
    names_to = "Category",
    values_to = "Count"
  ) %>%
  filter(Category != "Size") %>%
  mutate(
    Group = factor(Group, levels = levels(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts$Group)),
    Contrast = factor(Contrast, levels = levels(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts$Contrast)),
    DE = factor(DE, levels = levels(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts$DE)),
    Category = factor(Category, levels = colnames(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts)[4:ncol(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts)][colnames(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts)[4:ncol(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts)] != "Size"])
  )

ggplot(inter_strain_time_course_contrasts_enrichments, aes(x = Category, y = Count, fill = DE)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(Count == 0, NA, Count)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
    vjust = -0.4, size = 4
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("UP" = "red", "DOWN" = "blue")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("COG", "KEGG", "GO (BP)", "GO (MF)", "GO (CC)", "EC (1)", "EC (2)", "EC (3)")) +
  facet_grid(Contrast ~ Group) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 60), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Enrichment category", y = "Enrichments", fill = "DE")

title: GIL16 network
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps.
In the Hierarchical clustering section, we used all ρ values from 0, 10, 30 and 60 minute samples ​​to determine coexpression clusters. In this case, the goal is to create a network, and if we retain all values, the network will be fully connected and therefore uninformative. The propr package contains a function to determine the significance of proportionality using a built-in permutation procedure and estimate the false discovery rate (FDR). Although the code is not shown below due to its computationally intensive execution, to find a threshold where the FDR < 0.05, both positive and negative values ​​were considered and tests were performed to the second decimal place with up to 1000 permutation tests.
For the GIL16 networks, the selected threshold was 0.83, i.e., all coexpressions between -0.83 and 0.83 were considered null because they could be random. Subsequently, the rho values ​​were rounded to five decimal places, and the positive and negative coexpression matrices, along with their respective strength-associated matrices, were constructed. Since edge weights in networks are usually interpreted as distances between nodes, the positive matrix was reconstructed so that the weights were 1 - ρ. However, for the strength matrix, since this parameter is associated with the edge weight, the same conversion was performed again to calculate this parameter. For the negative coexpression matrix, since the values ​​are obviously negative, the conversion was performed as 1 + ρ, so that very strong negative coexpressions would tend towards 0 (although they would never be 0 in any case). For the strength-associated matrix, the conversion was the same as that performed for the strength-associated positive matrix.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[c(1:4934, 4968:5000), 25:36]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

rhos_mat <- rhos@matrix
threshold <- 0.83
rhos_mat[ rhos_mat > -threshold & rhos_mat < threshold ] <- 0
rhos_mat <- round(rhos_mat, 5)

pos_mat <- rhos_mat
pos_mat[pos_mat < threshold] <- 0
pos_mat <- ifelse(pos_mat != 0, 1 - pos_mat, pos_mat)
pos_mat_strength <- ifelse(pos_mat != 0, 1 - pos_mat, pos_mat)

neg_mat <- rhos_mat
neg_mat[neg_mat > -threshold] <- 0
neg_mat <- ifelse(neg_mat != 0, neg_mat + 1, neg_mat)
neg_mat_strength <- ifelse(neg_mat != 0, 1 - neg_mat, neg_mat)

title: GIL16 network
text:
Positive network

Full-network attributes
This section will display the main full-network attributes. While not very informative on their own, they will allow us to compare these metrics across different networks. An additional table is also included, showing the number of components in which the network is divided and the size (number of genes) of each component. In this case, we see that 85.10% of the genes (4227 out of 4967) are interconnected somehow. Conversely, 9.56% of the genes (475 out of 4967) are not coexpressed with any other genes. The remaining genes are distributed in components of intermediate sizes, i.e., they are not isolated, but are coexpressed with few genes.


Code
order_pos <- gorder(g_pos)
size_pos <- gsize(g_pos)
mean_degree_pos <- 2 * (size_pos/order_pos)
assortativity_pos <- assortativity_degree(g_pos)
density_pos <- edge_density(g_pos)
diameter_pos <- diameter(g_pos)
avg_path_length_pos <- mean_distance(g_pos)
ta_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "average")
tg_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "global")
ge_pos <- global_efficiency(g_pos)

df_pos <- data.frame(
  Order = order_pos,
  Size = size_pos,
  Mean_degree = round(mean_degree_pos, 2),
  Assortativity = round(assortativity_pos, 3),
  Density = round(density_pos, 3),
  Diameter = round(diameter_pos, 2),
  Average_path_length = round(avg_path_length_pos, 3),
  Average_clustering_coefficient = round(ta_pos, 3),
  Global_transitivity = round(tg_pos, 3),
  Global_efficiency = round(ge_pos, 2),
  row.names = "Positive network"
)

datatable(t(df_pos), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
components_pos_df <- as.data.frame(table(Size = table(components(g_pos)$membership)))
colnames(components_pos_df)[2] <- "Components"
components_pos_df <- components_pos_df[order(components_pos_df$Components), c("Components", "Size")]
datatable(components_pos_df, rownames = FALSE)








Per-node attributes


Code
degree_pos <- degree(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
strength_pos <- strength(g_pos_strength)
betweenness_pos <- betweenness(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
coreness_pos <- coreness(g_pos)
tl_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "local")
triangles_pos <- setNames(count_triangles(g_pos), V(g_pos)$name)
harmonic_centrality_pos <- harmonic_centrality(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
eig_pos <- eigen_centrality(g_pos)$vector
pr_pos <- page_rank(g_pos)$vector
apl_pos <- apl(g_pos)



Degree
The degree of a vertex is its most basic structural property, the number of its adjacent edges. In the lower histogram, which can also be called the degree distribution, we see how many genes have a given normalized degree (degree divided by the network size minus 1) in each interval. As is often the case in biological networks, most nodes have few connections and only a few have many connections, i.e., a small fraction of genes are coexpressed with many other genes.


Code
network_hist(degree_pos, xlab = "Degree")











Strength
The strength of a node is calculated by summing the edge weights of the adjacent edges for each node. While conceptually similar to degree, at the gene level it allows us to discern whether coexpressions with other genes are strong or weak, whereas in the case of degree, the attribute is more boolean, indicating whether or not a gene coexpression exists. Comparing strength distribution with degree distribution reveals a similar pattern, with few genes exhibiting high strength.


Code
network_hist(strength_pos, xlab = "Strength")











Betweenness
Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between other pairs of nodes in the network. In other words, it captures the extent to which a node acts as an intermediary in connecting different parts of the network. In most networks, the normalized betweenness distribution is highly uneven: the majority of nodes have very low betweenness, while only a few nodes concentrate high values. Nodes with high betweenness can be interpreted as bottlenecks, since many shortest paths rely on them; as a result, they may play a crucial role in controlling information flow, i.e., gene expression.


Code
network_hist(betweenness_pos, xlab = "Betweenness")











Coreness
The k-core of graph is a maximal subgraph in which each vertex has at least degree k. The coreness of a vertex is k if it belongs to the k-core but not to the (k+1)-core. In this case, we see three distinct groups: genes with low coreness, genes in intermediate k-cores and genes belonging to high k-cores. The first group will be genes that have few positive coexpressions with other genes, while the last group will be those closely related to each other.


Code
network_hist(coreness_pos, xlab = "Coreness")











Clustering coefficient (C)
The clustering coefficient (C) is a local metric that measures the level of clustering of the nodes. In this case, the distribution differs from a normal distribution, since there are many nodes with high C values. However, a high value does not necessarily reflect that a node is important, since it may be isolated nodes that, conceptually, form a perfect cluster, since they are grouped with each other. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(tl_pos, xlab = "Clustering coefficient (C)")











Triangles
Triangles reflect the number of triangles a node is part of, i.e., the number of sets of three nodes that a particular node belongs to. In this case, a distribution with high positive skewness is observed. In other words, few nodes form many triangles with other nodes.


Code
network_hist(triangles_pos, xlab = "Triangles")











Harmonic centrality
The harmonic centrality of a vertex is the mean inverse distance to all other vertices. The inverse distance to an unreachable vertex is considered to be zero. Since some nodes are disconnected, this metric allows us to determine how central their position is in the network. In this way, it is the equivalent of closeness for disconnected networks. In the histogram below, we can observe that most nodes have a harmonic centrality between 0 and 3, while only a few exceed 5. In other words, only a few genes occupy a very central position (i.e., are close to the rest) in the positive coexpression network.


Code
network_hist(harmonic_centrality_pos, xlab = "Harmonic centrality")











Eigenvector centrality
Eigenvector centrality measures how important a node is in a network by considering not only how many connections it has, but also how important its neighbors are. A node gets a high score if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. This reflects a recursive idea: being connected to important nodes makes a node more important in return. As a result, nodes with high eigenvector centrality tend to belong to dense groups or cliques, where many nodes are interconnected. From a mathematical perspective, these scores come from the main eigenvector of the network’s adjacency matrix, which captures the strongest overall pattern of connections in the network.
In the lower histogram, we observe that more than 93% of the nodes have a low eigenvector centrality, while less than 0.5% exceed a value of 0.8.


Code
network_hist(eig_pos, xlab = "Eigenvector centrality", n_intervals = 5)











PageRank
PageRank measures how central or important a node is based on the idea that connections from important nodes contribute more than connections from less important ones, even when the network has no direction. In an undirected network, PageRank works similarly to eigenvector centrality: a node has a high PageRank if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. The score can be interpreted as the probability that a random walker moving along edges (and occasionally jumping to a random node) will be found at that node. Therefore, nodes with high PageRank tend to lie in dense or well-connected parts of the network, rather than being isolated or peripheral.
In this case, the distribution is much more platykurtic than for eigenvector centrality, although there are still few genes with high values.


Code
network_hist(pr_pos, xlab = "PageRank", n_intervals = 5)











Average path length (L)
Average path length (also known as average shortest path length or characteristic path) for a node describes how far is, on average, from all other nodes in the network. It is computed as the mean of the shortest path distances from that node to every other reachable node. In simple terms, it tells us how many steps are typically needed to reach the rest of the network starting from this node. A small average path length indicates that the node is centrally located and can reach others efficiently, while a large value suggests that the node lies in a more peripheral position, requiring more intermediaries to connect to the rest of the network.
In this case, the distribution is heavily centered on values between 0.4 and 1. The most important nodes regarding this attribute are those with the lowest L values. Therefore, this attribute is expected to be negatively correlated with the other centrality values ​​obtained. In any case, this attribute is not particularly informative, since the nodes with lowest L values could be in low order components. Hence, this attribute should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(apl_pos, xlab = "Average path length (L)")












Per-edge attributes
Edge betweenness centrality measures how often an edge lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between pairs of nodes in the network. Conceptually, it quantifies the extent to which an edge acts as a bridge for connecting different parts of the network. Edges with high betweenness can be interpreted as critical links or bottlenecks, since their removal may strongly affect network connectivity or information flow. From a biological point of view, the edges could be interactions between proteins that, when eliminated, would alter the coexpression network.
The table below shows the edge betweenness values ​​for each pair of genes that exhibit positive coexpression. The highest values within each of the six gene-pair categories are obtained for the following combinations:

Chromosome–chromosome: 4-hydroxy-tetrahydrodipicolinate synthase (GBJ002_00959) gene with Xaa-Pro dipeptidase (GBJ002_01827) gene.
Chromosome–pBtic235: capsule synthesis protein CapA domain-containing protein (GBJ002_04558) gene with tRNA-Asn(gtt) (GBJ002_05160) gene.
Chromosome-GIL16: ArsR family transcriptional regulator (GBJ002_03413) gene with DNA, RNA and nucleotide metabolism (GIL16_07) gene.
pBtic235–pBtic235: tRNA-Asn(gtt) (GBJ002_05160) gene with tRNA-Leu(tag) (GBJ002_05161) gene.
pBtic235-GIL16: recombinase RecA (GBJ002_05184) gene with integration and excision (GIL16_34) gene.
GIL16-GIL16: two head and packaging (GIL16_08 and GIL16_35) genes.



Code
edge_betweenness_pos <- igraph::as_data_frame(g_pos, what = "edges")
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id <- seq_len(size_pos)
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_betweenness <- edge_betweenness(g_pos)
edge_betweenness_pos <- edge_betweenness_pos[order(edge_betweenness_pos$edge_betweenness, decreasing = TRUE), ]
rownames(edge_betweenness_pos) <- as.character(edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id)
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id <- NULL

datatable(edge_betweenness_pos, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Gene 1", "Gene 2", "Weight (1 - ρ)", "Edge betweenness"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "weight", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "edge_betweenness", digits = 2)

title: GIL16 network
text:
Negative network

Full-network attributes
This section will display the main full-network attributes. While not very informative on their own, they will allow us to compare these metrics across different networks. An additional table is also included, showing the number of components in which the network is divided and the size (number of genes) of each component. In this case, we see that 65.21% of the genes (3239 out of 4967) are interconnected somehow. Conversely, 31.77% of the genes (1578 out of 4967) are not negatively coexpressed with any other genes. The remaining genes are distributed in components of intermediate sizes, i.e., they are not isolated, but are coexpressed with few genes.


Code
order_neg <- gorder(g_neg)
size_neg <- gsize(g_neg)
mean_degree_neg <- 2 * (size_neg/order_neg)
assortativity_neg <- assortativity_degree(g_neg)
density_neg <- edge_density(g_neg)
diameter_neg <- diameter(g_neg)
avg_path_length_neg <- mean_distance(g_neg)
ta_neg <- transitivity(g_neg, type = "average")
tg_neg <- transitivity(g_neg, type = "global")
ge_neg <- global_efficiency(g_neg)

df_neg <- data.frame(
  Order = order_neg,
  Size = size_neg,
  Mean_degree = round(mean_degree_neg, 2),
  Assortativity = round(assortativity_neg, 3),
  Density = round(density_neg, 3),
  Diameter = round(diameter_neg, 2),
  Average_path_length = round(avg_path_length_neg, 3),
  Average_clustering_coefficient = ta_neg,
  Global_transitivity = tg_neg,
  Global_efficiency = round(ge_neg, 2),
  row.names = "Negative network"
)

datatable(t(df_neg), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
components_neg_df <- as.data.frame(table(Size = table(components(g_neg)$membership)))
colnames(components_neg_df)[2] <- "Components"
components_neg_df <- components_neg_df[order(components_neg_df$Components), c("Components", "Size")]
datatable(components_neg_df, rownames = FALSE)








Per-node attributes


Code
degree_neg <- degree(g_neg, normalized = TRUE)
strength_neg <- strength(g_neg_strength)
betweenness_neg <- betweenness(g_neg, normalized = TRUE)
coreness_neg <- coreness(g_neg)
tl_neg <- transitivity(g_neg, type = "local")
triangles_neg <- setNames(count_triangles(g_neg), V(g_neg)$name)
harmonic_centrality_neg <- harmonic_centrality(g_neg, normalized = TRUE)
eig_neg <- eigen_centrality(g_neg)$vector
pr_neg <- page_rank(g_neg)$vector
apl_neg <- apl(g_neg)



Degree
The degree of a vertex is its most basic structural property, the number of its adjacent edges. In the lower histogram, which can also be called the degree distribution, we see how many genes have a given normalized degree (degree divided by the network size minus 1) in each interval. As is often the case in biological networks, most nodes have few connections and only a few have many connections, i.e., a small fraction of genes are negatively coexpressed with many other genes.


Code
network_hist(degree_neg, xlab = "Degree")











Strength
The strength of a node is calculated by summing the edge weights of the adjacent edges for each node. While conceptually similar to degree, at the gene level it allows us to discern whether coexpressions with other genes are strong or weak, whereas in the case of degree, the attribute is more boolean, indicating whether or not a gene coexpression exists. Comparing strength distribution with degree distribution reveals a similar pattern, with few genes exhibiting high strength.


Code
network_hist(strength_neg, xlab = "Strength")











Betweenness
Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between other pairs of nodes in the network. In other words, it captures the extent to which a node acts as an intermediary in connecting different parts of the network. In most networks, the normalized betweenness distribution is highly uneven: the majority of nodes have very low betweenness, while only a few nodes concentrate high values. Nodes with high betweenness can be interpreted as bottlenecks, since many shortest paths rely on them; as a result, they may play a crucial role in controlling information flow, i.e., gene expression.


Code
network_hist(betweenness_neg, xlab = "Betweenness")











Coreness
The k-core of graph is a maximal subgraph in which each vertex has at least degree k. The coreness of a vertex is k if it belongs to the k-core but not to the (k+1)-core. In this case, we see three distinct groups: genes with low coreness, genes in intermediate k-cores and genes belonging to high k-cores. The first group will be genes that have few negative coexpressions with other genes, while the last and smallest group will be those closely related to each other.


Code
network_hist(coreness_neg, xlab = "Coreness")











Clustering coefficient (C)
The clustering coefficient (C) is a local metric that measures the level of clustering of the nodes. In this case, the distribution is uniform, with all nodes that have two or more neighbours showing C = 0. This is logical by definition, although it is better understood with the concept of triangles, explained below.


Code
sprintf("All %d nodes that have two or more neighbours show C = %s.", as.integer(table(tl_neg)), names(table(tl_neg)))


[1] "All 2839 nodes that have two or more neighbours show C = 0."




Triangles
Triangles reflect the number of triangles a node is part of, i.e., the number of sets of three nodes that a particular node belongs to. In this case, the distribution is uniform, since no node is included in any triangle. This is logical by definition, because if the interactions are negative, there cannot be triangles. Assuming three nodes A, B, and C, if A is negatively coexpressed with B and B is negatively coexpressed with C, then B could never be negatively coexpressed with C. Therefore, we will never obtain triangles in negative coexpression networks.


Code
sprintf("All %d nodes have %s triangles.", as.integer(table(triangles_neg)), names(table(triangles_neg)))


[1] "All 4967 nodes have 0 triangles."




Harmonic centrality
The harmonic centrality of a vertex is the mean inverse distance to all other vertices. The inverse distance to an unreachable vertex is considered to be zero. Since some nodes are disconnected, this metric allows us to determine how central their position is in the network. In this way, it is the equivalent of closeness for disconnected networks. In the histogram below, we can observe that most nodes have a harmonic centrality between 0.6 and 2 (although there is also a significant portion of nodes concentrated in values ​​close to 0, i.e., nodes not in the major component), while only five nodes exceed a value of 2.2. In other words, only a few genes occupy a very central position (i.e., are close to the rest) in the negative coexpression network.


Code
network_hist(harmonic_centrality_neg, xlab = "Harmonic centrality")











Eigenvector centrality
Eigenvector centrality measures how important a node is in a network by considering not only how many connections it has, but also how important its neighbors are. A node gets a high score if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. This reflects a recursive idea: being connected to important nodes makes a node more important in return. As a result, nodes with high eigenvector centrality tend to belong to dense groups or cliques, where many nodes are interconnected. From a mathematical perspective, these scores come from the main eigenvector of the network’s adjacency matrix, which captures the strongest overall pattern of connections in the network.
In the lower histogram, we observe that almost 90% of the nodes have a low eigenvector centrality, while only three genes exceed a value of 0.9.


Code
network_hist(eig_neg, xlab = "Eigenvector centrality")











PageRank
PageRank measures how central or important a node is based on the idea that connections from important nodes contribute more than connections from less important ones, even when the network has no direction. In an undirected network, PageRank works similarly to eigenvector centrality: a node has a high PageRank if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. The score can be interpreted as the probability that a random walker moving along edges (and occasionally jumping to a random node) will be found at that node. Therefore, nodes with high PageRank tend to lie in dense or well-connected parts of the network, rather than being isolated or peripheral.
In this case, the distribution is similar to the eigenvector centrality one and there are few genes with high values.


Code
network_hist(pr_neg, xlab = "PageRank")











Average path length (L)
Average path length (also known as average shortest path length or characteristic path) for a node describes how far is, on average, from all other nodes in the network. It is computed as the mean of the shortest path distances from that node to every other reachable node. In simple terms, it tells us how many steps are typically needed to reach the rest of the network starting from this node. A small average path length indicates that the node is centrally located and can reach others efficiently, while a large value suggests that the node lies in a more peripheral position, requiring more intermediaries to connect to the rest of the network.
In this case, the distribution is heavily centered on values between 0.4 and 0.6. The most important nodes regarding this attribute are those with the lowest L values. Therefore, this attribute is expected to be negatively correlated with the other centrality values ​​obtained. In any case, this attribute is not particularly informative, since the nodes with lowest L values could be in low order components. Hence, this attribute should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(apl_neg, xlab = "Average path length (L)")












Per-edge attributes
Edge betweenness centrality measures how often an edge lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between pairs of nodes in the network. Conceptually, it quantifies the extent to which an edge acts as a bridge for connecting different parts of the network. Edges with high betweenness can be interpreted as critical links or bottlenecks, since their removal may strongly affect network connectivity or information flow. From a biological point of view, the edges could be interactions between proteins that, when eliminated, would alter the coexpression network.
The table below shows the edge betweenness values ​​for each pair of genes that exhibit negative coexpression. The highest values within each of the six gene-pair categories are obtained for the following combinations:

Chromosome–chromosome: de novo transcript (GBJ002-DNT_019) gene with ketol-acid reductoisomerase (GBJ002_04146) gene.
Chromosome–pBtic235: FeS cluster biogenesis domain-containing protein (GBJ002_01978) gene with hypothetical protein (GBJ002_05234) gene.
Chromosome-GIL16: N-acetyltransferase domain-containing protein (GBJ002_00265) gene with integration and excision (GIL16_34) gene.
pBtic235–pBtic235: sigma-70 family RNA polymerase sigma factor (GBJ002_05270) gene with SMI1/KNR4 family protein (GBJ002_05285) gene.

No negative coexpressions were found between pBtic235-GIL16 and GIL16-GIL16 gene-pair categories.


Code
edge_betweenness_neg <- igraph::as_data_frame(g_neg, what = "edges")
edge_betweenness_neg$edge_id <- seq_len(size_neg)
edge_betweenness_neg$edge_betweenness <- edge_betweenness(g_neg)
edge_betweenness_neg <- edge_betweenness_neg[order(edge_betweenness_neg$edge_betweenness, decreasing = TRUE), ]
rownames(edge_betweenness_neg) <- as.character(edge_betweenness_neg$edge_id)
edge_betweenness_neg$edge_id <- NULL

datatable(edge_betweenness_neg, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Gene 1", "Gene 2", "Weight (1 - ρ)", "Edge betweenness"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "weight", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "edge_betweenness", digits = 2)

title: GIL16 network
text:
Networks comparison
This section compares the full-network attributes obtained for positive and negative coexpression networks. Since the same nodes (genes) are used in both cases, the order (number of genes) is expected to be the same. However, the size of the positive network is larger than that of the negative network, indicating more positive than negative coexpressions. Similarly, the mean degree of the positive network is higher.
Regarding the number of components, in both networks most nodes belong to a single component. However, the negative network has a higher proportion of isolated nodes.


Code
datatable(t(rbind(df_pos, df_neg)), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
datatable(components_pos_df, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive network components")






Code
datatable(components_neg_df, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative network components")






The tables below show the values ​​of each per-node attribute for each of the nodes in the positive and negative networks.


Code
data_pos <- build_nodes_data(data = list(
      Degree = degree_pos,
      Strength = strength_pos,
      Betweenness = betweenness_pos,
      Coreness = coreness_pos,
      Clustering_coefficient = tl_pos,
      Triangles = triangles_pos,
      Harmonic_centrality = harmonic_centrality_pos,
      Eigenvector_centrality = eig_pos,
      PageRank = pr_pos,
      Average_path_length = apl_pos
))

data_neg <- build_nodes_data(data = list(
      Degree = degree_neg,
      Strength = strength_neg,
      Betweenness = betweenness_neg,
      Coreness = coreness_neg,
      Harmonic_centrality = harmonic_centrality_neg,
      Eigenvector_centrality = eig_neg,
      PageRank = pr_neg,
      Average_path_length = apl_neg
))

datatable(data_pos, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Gene", "Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Clustering coefficient (C)", "Triangles",
               "Harmonic centrality", "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank", "Average path length (L)"),
  caption = "Positive network per-node attributes",
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Degree", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Strength", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Betweenness", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Clustering_coefficient", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Harmonic_centrality", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Eigenvector_centrality", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "PageRank", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Average_path_length", digits = 3)






Code
datatable(data_neg, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Gene", "Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Harmonic centrality",
               "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank", "Average path length (L)"),
  caption = "Negative network per-node attributes",
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Degree", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Strength", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Betweenness", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Harmonic_centrality", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Eigenvector_centrality", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "PageRank", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Average_path_length", digits = 3)






Below, two interactive heatmaps are shown, one for each network, displaying the Spearman correlations (ρ) between per-node attributes. Each correlation is computed using only those nodes for which both attributes are available, so the set of nodes contributing to each cell may vary across comparisons, reflecting the presence of missing values while maximizing statistical power. In the positive network, degree shows very high correlation with several measures, such as strength, coreness, harmonic centrality, PageRank and the number of triangles, indicating that these attributes largely capture the same topological dimension. In contrast, betweenness centrality exhibits only moderate association with degree, suggesting that it provides complementary information related to global network mediation. Eigenvector centrality shows an intermediate level of association with degree, indicating partial redundancy. A similar pattern is observed in the negative network, where degree is strongly correlated with most connectivity-based measures, including eigenvector centrality, reflecting the more centralized architecture of this network. In this case, betweenness centrality again shows substantially lower correlation with degree, highlighting its role as a distinct descriptor of node importance. Taken together, these results indicate that many commonly used attributes are highly redundant within each network.


Code
cor_mat_pos <- cor(data_pos[, 2:ncol(data_pos)], use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")
cor_mat_neg <- cor(data_neg[, 2:ncol(data_neg)], use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")

p_cor_mat_pos <- heatmaply(
      round(cor_mat_pos, 3),
      limits = c(-1, 1),
      dendrogram = "none",
      main = "Positive network nodes attributes associations",
      label_names = c("Row", "Column", "ρ"))

p_cor_mat_neg <- heatmaply(
      round(cor_mat_neg, 3),
      limits = c(-1, 1),
      dendrogram = "none",
      main = "Positive network nodes attributes associations",
      label_names = c("Row", "Column", "ρ"))

p_cor_mat_pos






Code
p_cor_mat_neg

title: GBJ002 network
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps.
In the Hierarchical clustering section, we used all ρ values from 0, 10, 30 and 60 minute samples ​​to determine coexpression clusters. In this case, the goal is to create a network, and if we retain all values, the network will be fully connected and therefore uninformative. The propr package contains a function to determine the significance of proportionality using a built-in permutation procedure and estimate the false discovery rate (FDR). Although the code is not shown below due to its computationally intensive execution, to find a threshold where the FDR < 0.05, both positive and negative values ​​were considered and tests were performed to the second decimal place with up to 1000 permutation tests.
For the GBJ002 networks, the selected threshold was 0.74, i.e., all coexpressions between -0.74 and 0.74 were considered null because they could be random. Subsequently, the rho values ​​were rounded to five decimal places, and the positive and negative coexpression matrices, along with their respective strength-associated matrices, were constructed. Since edge weights in networks are usually interpreted as distances between nodes, the positive matrix was reconstructed so that the weights were 1 - ρ. However, for the strength matrix, since this parameter is associated with the edge weight, the same conversion was performed again to calculate this parameter. For the negative coexpression matrix, since the values ​​are obviously negative, the conversion was performed as 1 + ρ, so that very strong negative coexpressions would tend towards 0 (although they would never be 0 in any case). For the strength-associated matrix, the conversion was the same as that performed for the strength-associated positive matrix.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[1:4934, 1:12]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

rhos_mat <- rhos@matrix
threshold <- 0.74
rhos_mat[ rhos_mat > -threshold & rhos_mat < threshold ] <- 0
rhos_mat <- round(rhos_mat, 5)

pos_mat <- rhos_mat
pos_mat[pos_mat < threshold] <- 0
pos_mat <- ifelse(pos_mat != 0, 1 - pos_mat, pos_mat)
pos_mat_strength <- ifelse(pos_mat != 0, 1 - pos_mat, pos_mat)

neg_mat <- rhos_mat
neg_mat[neg_mat > -threshold] <- 0
neg_mat <- ifelse(neg_mat != 0, neg_mat + 1, neg_mat)
neg_mat_strength <- ifelse(neg_mat != 0, 1 - neg_mat, neg_mat)

title: GBJ002 network
text:
Positive network

Full-network attributes
This section will display the main full-network attributes. While not very informative on their own, they will allow us to compare these metrics across different networks. An additional table is also included, showing the number of components in which the network is divided and the size (number of genes) of each component. In this case, we see that 96.17% of the genes (4745 out of 4934) are interconnected somehow. Conversely, 3.16% of the genes (156 out of 4934) are not coexpressed with any other genes. The remaining genes are distributed in components of intermediate sizes, i.e., they are not isolated, but are coexpressed with few genes.


Code
order_pos <- gorder(g_pos)
size_pos <- gsize(g_pos)
mean_degree_pos <- 2 * (size_pos/order_pos)
assortativity_pos <- assortativity_degree(g_pos)
density_pos <- edge_density(g_pos)
diameter_pos <- diameter(g_pos)
avg_path_length_pos <- mean_distance(g_pos)
ta_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "average")
tg_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "global")
ge_pos <- global_efficiency(g_pos)

df_pos <- data.frame(
  Order = order_pos,
  Size = size_pos,
  Mean_degree = round(mean_degree_pos, 2),
  Assortativity = round(assortativity_pos, 3),
  Density = round(density_pos, 3),
  Diameter = round(diameter_pos, 2),
  Average_path_length = round(avg_path_length_pos, 3),
  Average_clustering_coefficient = round(ta_pos, 3),
  Global_transitivity = round(tg_pos, 3),
  Global_efficiency = round(ge_pos, 2),
  row.names = "Positive network"
)

datatable(t(df_pos), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
components_pos_df <- as.data.frame(table(Size = table(components(g_pos)$membership)))
colnames(components_pos_df)[2] <- "Components"
components_pos_df <- components_pos_df[order(components_pos_df$Components), c("Components", "Size")]
datatable(components_pos_df, rownames = FALSE)








Per-node attributes


Code
degree_pos <- degree(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
strength_pos <- strength(g_pos_strength)
betweenness_pos <- betweenness(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
coreness_pos <- coreness(g_pos)
tl_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "local")
triangles_pos <- setNames(count_triangles(g_pos), V(g_pos)$name)
harmonic_centrality_pos <- harmonic_centrality(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
eig_pos <- eigen_centrality(g_pos)$vector
pr_pos <- page_rank(g_pos)$vector
apl_pos <- apl(g_pos)



Degree
The degree of a vertex is its most basic structural property, the number of its adjacent edges. In the lower histogram, which can also be called the degree distribution, we see how many genes have a given normalized degree (degree divided by the network size minus 1) in each interval. As is often the case in biological networks, most nodes have few connections and only a few have many connections, i.e., a small fraction of genes are coexpressed with many other genes.


Code
network_hist(degree_pos, xlab = "Degree")











Strength
The strength of a node is calculated by summing the edge weights of the adjacent edges for each node. While conceptually similar to degree, at the gene level it allows us to discern whether coexpressions with other genes are strong or weak, whereas in the case of degree, the attribute is more boolean, indicating whether or not a gene coexpression exists. Comparing strength distribution with degree distribution reveals a similar pattern, with few genes exhibiting high strength.


Code
network_hist(strength_pos, xlab = "Strength")











Betweenness
Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between other pairs of nodes in the network. In other words, it captures the extent to which a node acts as an intermediary in connecting different parts of the network. In most networks, the normalized betweenness distribution is highly uneven: the majority of nodes have very low betweenness, while only a few nodes concentrate high values. Nodes with high betweenness can be interpreted as bottlenecks, since many shortest paths rely on them; as a result, they may play a crucial role in controlling information flow, i.e., gene expression.


Code
network_hist(betweenness_pos, xlab = "Betweenness")











Coreness
The k-core of graph is a maximal subgraph in which each vertex has at least degree k. The coreness of a vertex is k if it belongs to the k-core but not to the (k+1)-core. In this case, we see three distinct groups: genes with low coreness, genes in intermediate k-cores and genes belonging to high k-cores. The first group will be genes that have few positive coexpressions with other genes, while the last group will be those closely related to each other.


Code
network_hist(coreness_pos, xlab = "Coreness", n_intervals = 5)











Clustering coefficient (C)
The clustering coefficient (C) is a local metric that measures the level of clustering of the nodes. In this case, the distribution approximates a normal distribution, although with longer tails. In any case, it is observed that there are few genes with a C > 0.9. However, a high value does not necessarily reflect that a node is important, since it may be isolated nodes that, conceptually, form a perfect cluster, since they are grouped with each other. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(tl_pos, xlab = "Clustering coefficient (C)")











Triangles
Triangles reflect the number of triangles a node is part of, i.e., the number of sets of three nodes that a particular node belongs to. In this case, a distribution with high positive skewness is observed. In other words, few nodes form many triangles with other nodes.


Code
network_hist(triangles_pos, xlab = "Triangles")











Harmonic centrality
The harmonic centrality of a vertex is the mean inverse distance to all other vertices. The inverse distance to an unreachable vertex is considered to be zero. Since some nodes are disconnected, this metric allows us to determine how central their position is in the network. In this way, it is the equivalent of closeness for disconnected networks. In the histogram below, we can observe that most nodes have a harmonic centrality between 1 and 2.5, while only a few exceed 3.5. In other words, only a few genes occupy a very central position (i.e., are close to the rest) in the positive coexpression network.


Code
network_hist(harmonic_centrality_pos, xlab = "Harmonic centrality")











Eigenvector centrality
Eigenvector centrality measures how important a node is in a network by considering not only how many connections it has, but also how important its neighbors are. A node gets a high score if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. This reflects a recursive idea: being connected to important nodes makes a node more important in return. As a result, nodes with high eigenvector centrality tend to belong to dense groups or cliques, where many nodes are interconnected. From a mathematical perspective, these scores come from the main eigenvector of the network’s adjacency matrix, which captures the strongest overall pattern of connections in the network.
In the lower histogram, we observe that more than 80% of the nodes have a low eigenvector centrality, while only 3.22% exceed a value of 0.8.


Code
network_hist(eig_pos, xlab = "Eigenvector centrality", n_intervals = 5)











PageRank
PageRank measures how central or important a node is based on the idea that connections from important nodes contribute more than connections from less important ones, even when the network has no direction. In an undirected network, PageRank works similarly to eigenvector centrality: a node has a high PageRank if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. The score can be interpreted as the probability that a random walker moving along edges (and occasionally jumping to a random node) will be found at that node. Therefore, nodes with high PageRank tend to lie in dense or well-connected parts of the network, rather than being isolated or peripheral.
In this case, the distribution is much more platykurtic than for eigenvector centrality, although there are still few genes with high values.


Code
network_hist(pr_pos, xlab = "PageRank", n_intervals = 5)











Average path length (L)
Average path length (also known as average shortest path length or characteristic path) for a node describes how far is, on average, from all other nodes in the network. It is computed as the mean of the shortest path distances from that node to every other reachable node. In simple terms, it tells us how many steps are typically needed to reach the rest of the network starting from this node. A small average path length indicates that the node is centrally located and can reach others efficiently, while a large value suggests that the node lies in a more peripheral position, requiring more intermediaries to connect to the rest of the network.
In this case, the distribution is heavily centered on values between 0.4 and 1. The most important nodes regarding this attribute are those with the lowest L values. Therefore, this attribute is expected to be negatively correlated with the other centrality values ​​obtained. In any case, this attribute is not particularly informative, since the nodes with lowest L values could be in low order components. Hence, this attribute should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(apl_pos, xlab = "Average path length (L)")












Per-edge attributes
Edge betweenness centrality measures how often an edge lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between pairs of nodes in the network. Conceptually, it quantifies the extent to which an edge acts as a bridge for connecting different parts of the network. Edges with high betweenness can be interpreted as critical links or bottlenecks, since their removal may strongly affect network connectivity or information flow. From a biological point of view, the edges could be interactions between proteins that, when eliminated, would alter the coexpression network.
The table below shows the edge betweenness values ​​for each pair of genes that exhibit positive coexpression. The highest values within each of the three gene-pair categories are obtained for the following combinations:

Chromosome–chromosome: two heme uptake protein IsdC (GBJ002_01761 and GBJ002_01763) genes.
Chromosome–pBtic235: heme uptake protein IsdC (GBJ002_01763) gene with hypothetical protein (GBJ002_05067) gene.
pBtic235–pBtic235: nuclease (GBJ002_05044) gene with fibronectin type‑III domain-containing protein (GBJ002_05313) gene.



Code
edge_betweenness_pos <- igraph::as_data_frame(g_pos, what = "edges")
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id <- seq_len(size_pos)
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_betweenness <- edge_betweenness(g_pos)
edge_betweenness_pos <- edge_betweenness_pos[order(edge_betweenness_pos$edge_betweenness, decreasing = TRUE), ]
rownames(edge_betweenness_pos) <- as.character(edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id)
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id <- NULL

datatable(edge_betweenness_pos, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Gene 1", "Gene 2", "Weight (1 - ρ)", "Edge betweenness"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "weight", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "edge_betweenness", digits = 2)

The remaining genes are distributed in components of size 2, i.e., they are coexpressed with a single other gene.

As is often the case in biological networks, most nodes have few connections and only a few have many connections, i.e., a small fraction of genes are negatively coexpressed with many other genes.

The strength of a node is calculated by summing the edge weights of the adjacent edges for each node. Comparing strength distribution with degree distribution reveals a similar pattern, with few genes exhibiting high strength.

Betweenness
Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between other pairs of nodes in the network. In other words, it captures the extent to which a node acts as an intermediary in connecting different parts of the network. In most networks, the normalized betweenness distribution is highly uneven: the majority of nodes have very low betweenness, while only a few nodes concentrate high values. Nodes with high betweenness can be interpreted as bottlenecks, since many shortest paths rely on them; as a result, they may play a crucial role in controlling information flow, i.e., gene expression.

network_hist(betweenness_neg, xlab = "Betweenness")











Coreness
The k-core of graph is a maximal subgraph in which each vertex has at least degree k. The coreness of a vertex is k if it belongs to the k-core but not to the (k+1)-core. In this case, we see three distinct groups: genes with low coreness, genes in intermediate k-cores and genes belonging to high k-cores. The first group will be genes that have few negative coexpressions with other genes, while the last group will be those closely related to each other.


Code
network_hist(coreness_neg, xlab = "Coreness")











Clustering coefficient (C)
The clustering coefficient (C) is a local metric that measures the level of clustering of the nodes. In this case, the distribution is uniform, with all nodes that have two or more neighbours showing C = 0. This is logical by definition, although it is better understood with the concept of triangles, explained below.


Code
sprintf("All %d nodes that have two or more neighbours show C = %s.", as.integer(table(tl_neg)), names(table(tl_neg)))


[1] "All 4372 nodes that have two or more neighbours show C = 0."




Triangles
Triangles reflect the number of triangles a node is part of, i.e., the number of sets of three nodes that a particular node belongs to. In this case, the distribution is uniform, since no node is included in any triangle. This is logical by definition, because if the interactions are negative, there cannot be triangles. Assuming three nodes A, B and C, if A is negatively coexpressed with B and B is negatively coexpressed with C, then B could never be negatively coexpressed with C. Therefore, we will never obtain triangles in negative coexpression networks.


Code
sprintf("All %d nodes have %s triangles.", as.integer(table(triangles_neg)), names(table(triangles_neg)))


[1] "All 4934 nodes have 0 triangles."




Harmonic centrality
The harmonic centrality of a vertex is the mean inverse distance to all other vertices. The inverse distance to an unreachable vertex is considered to be zero. Since some nodes are disconnected, this metric allows us to determine how central their position is in the network. In this way, it is the equivalent of closeness for disconnected networks. In the histogram below, we can observe that most nodes have a harmonic centrality between 1 and 3, while only 29 nodes exceed a value of 4. In other words, only a few genes occupy a very central position (i.e., are close to the rest) in the negative coexpression network.


Code

Eigenvector centrality
Eigenvector centrality measures how important a node is in a network by considering not only how many connections it has, but also how important its neighbors are. A node gets a high score if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. This reflects a recursive idea: being connected to important nodes makes a node more important in return. As a result, nodes with high eigenvector centrality tend to belong to dense groups or cliques, where many nodes are interconnected. From a mathematical perspective, these scores come from the main eigenvector of the network’s adjacency matrix, which captures the strongest overall pattern of connections in the network.
In the lower histogram, we observe that almost 60% of the nodes have a low eigenvector centrality, while only 1.54% exceed a value of 0.9.


Code

PageRank
PageRank measures how central or important a node is based on the idea that connections from important nodes contribute more than connections from less important ones, even when the network has no direction. In an undirected network, PageRank works similarly to eigenvector centrality: a node has a high PageRank if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. The score can be interpreted as the probability that a random walker moving along edges (and occasionally jumping to a random node) will be found at that node. Therefore, nodes with high PageRank tend to lie in dense or well-connected parts of the network, rather than being isolated or peripheral.
In this case, the distribution is much more platykurtic than for eigenvector centrality, although there are still few genes with high values.


Code

Average path length (L)
Average path length (also known as average shortest path length or characteristic path) for a node describes how far is, on average, from all other nodes in the network. It is computed as the mean of the shortest path distances from that node to every other reachable node. In simple terms, it tells us how many steps are typically needed to reach the rest of the network starting from this node. A small average path length indicates that the node is centrally located and can reach others efficiently, while a large value suggests that the node lies in a more peripheral position, requiring more intermediaries to connect to the rest of the network.

Therefore, this attribute is expected to be negatively correlated with the other centrality values ​​obtained. In any case, this attribute is not particularly informative, since the nodes with lowest L values could be in low order components. Hence, this attribute should be interpreted with caution.

Per-edge attributes
Edge betweenness centrality measures how often an edge lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between pairs of nodes in the network. Conceptually, it quantifies the extent to which an edge acts as a bridge for connecting different parts of the network. Edges with high betweenness can be interpreted as critical links or bottlenecks, since their removal may strongly affect network connectivity or information flow. From a biological point of view, the edges could be interactions between proteins that, when eliminated, would alter the coexpression network.
The table below shows the edge betweenness values ​​for each pair of genes that exhibit negative coexpression. The highest values within each of the three gene-pair categories are obtained for the following combinations:

title: GIL16 hierarchical clustering
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps. In this case, we used rho proportionality to measure coexpression among GBJ002 (chromosome and pBtic235) and GIL16 genes and GIL16 samples at time points 0, 10, 30 and 60, excluding 60-mock samples, since they are not induced by MMC and may introduce noise.

pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[c(1:4934, 4968:5000), 25:36]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

title: GIL16 hierarchical clustering
text:
COG
Regarding COG categories, 11 clusters show significant enrichment comprising 9 distinct terms. Among the most notable findings are GIL16_C071, which is enriched in genes related to cell cycle control, cell division, chromosome partitioning (D), and GIL16_C019, which includes 5 genes involved in amino acid transport and metabolism (E).

title: GBJ002 hierarchical clustering
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps. In this case, we used rho proportionality to measure coexpression among GBJ002 (chromosome and pBtic235) genes and GBJ002 samples at time points 0, 10, 30 and 60, excluding 60-mock samples, since they are not induced by MMC and may introduce noise.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[1:4934, 1:12]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

Regarding COG categories, 9 clusters show significant enrichment comprising 8 distinct terms. Among the most notable findings are GBJ002_C133, which is enriched in genes related to cell motility (N), and GBJ002_C029c, which includes 23 genes involved in translation, ribosomal structure and biogenesis (J).

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 60 vs 60-mock contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, name = "MMC_TRUE_vs_FALSE", lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 707 upregulated and 404 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 5023 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 707, 14%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 404, 8%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 0)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category / Homologue", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we observed positive enrichment in processes mainly primarily to DNA, amino acids biosynthesis and cellular response to stimuli. Negative enrichment was found in processes associated with amino acids, cellular respiration and tRNA aminoacylation.
Regarding MF, we found positive enrichment in catalytic and ATP-dependent activities acting on DNA. Negative enrichment was associated to diverse functions, such as binding of ions, vitamins and small molecules, among others.
Regarding CC, negative enrichments were only found in cytoplasm and intracellular anatomical structure components.



Code
go_bp_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "BP")
go_bp_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "BP")

go_mf_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "MF")
go_mf_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "MF")

go_cc_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "CC")
go_cc_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "CC")

EC

In the first, ligases (6) number was enriched due to 23 downregulated ligases genes, among which are 11 tRNA ligases genes. In the second, 1.6 level was enriched by 6 upregulated genes.
