title: Time points 30 vs 0 contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 30 vs 0 contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Time_30_vs_0", "MMC_TRUE_vs_FALSE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 277 upregulated and 312 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 293 genes were discarded due to low counts, probably because they are expressed in latter times or in the absence of GIL16. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 5023 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 277, 5.5%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 312, 6.2%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 293, 5.8%
(mean count < 3)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category / Homologue", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

In the case of the former, they were mainly related to metabolism, but also to replication and repair processes, specifically nucleotide excision repair, due to GBJ002_01735 (excinuclease ABC subunit C), GBJ002_02363 (excinuclease ABC subunit UvrA), GBJ002_02364 (excinuclease ABC subunit B), GBJ002_03098 (DNA helicase PcrA), GBJ002_03099 (NAD-dependent DNA ligase LigA) and GBJ002_03820 (RNA polymerase recycling motor HelD) genes. In the case of the latter, it resulted in depletion of pathways related to amino acids and membrane transport by ABC transporters.

title: Time points 30 vs 0 contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we observed positive enrichment in processes related to stress response and carbohydrates and nucleotides metabolism, while negative enrichment was associated to transport pathways and metabolic processes of dicarboxylic acids.
Regarding MF, we found positive enrichment in processes related to energy and transmembrane transporter activity of protons and inorganic and monoatomic ions, while that of oganic anions and organic and carboxylic acids was depleted. This could suggest a change in membrane potential.

According to BP, positive enrichment was mainly related to amino acids, carbohydrates and nucleic acids metabolism, while negative enrichment was found in organic acids, oxoacids and other amino acids processes.

title: Time points 60 vs 0 contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 60 vs 0 contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Time_60_vs_0", "MMC_TRUE_vs_FALSE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 743 upregulated and 542 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 5023 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05

LFC < -0.58 (down) : 542, 11%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 0)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results

          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category / Homologue", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Time points 60 vs 0 contrast
text:
KEGG
According to KEGG categories, we found positive and negative enrichments. In the case of the former, they were mainly related to metabolism, but also to replication and repair processes, specifically nucleotide excision repair, mismatch repair and DNA replication. In the case of the latter, it resulted in depletion of less metabolic pathways than in previous contrast. Interestingly, both positive and negative enrichment appear in amino acid metabolism, although the former is concentrated in arginine biosynthesis, alanine, aspartate and glutamate metabolism, and valine, leucine and isoleucine degradation, while the latter is concentrated in valine, leucine and isoleucine biosynthesis and biosynthesis of amino acids. This could be interpreted as a shift in amino acid metabolism toward GIL01 proteins, since the formation of certain amino acids is clearly favoured.


Code
kegg_up <- kegg_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes)
kegg_down <- kegg_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes)

  formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3)

title: Time points 60 vs 0 contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we observed positive enrichment in processes related to pyrimidine metabolism, DNA damage and amino acid metabolism, among others. Negative enrichment was found in metabolic processes associated with branched-chain amino acids, i.e., valine, leucine and isoleucine.
Regarding MF, we only found positive enrichment in functions related to energy metabolism and transporter and DNA-associated activities.

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 60 vs 60-mock contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, name = "MMC_TRUE_vs_FALSE", lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 718 upregulated and 550 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 718, 14%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 550, 11%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 3)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
COG
According to COG categories, we found positive enrichment in replication, recombination and repair (L) and nucleotide transport and metabolism (F) and negative enrichment in cell motility (N). Interestingly, there are numerous downregulated flagellar protein genes, so B. thuringiensis swarming motility could also be affected in the absence of tectivirus (Gillis & Mahillon, 2014). This contrast reflects that the impairment of cell motility is not due to bacterial growth, but is affected by induction with MMC.

KEGG
According to KEGG categories, we found positive and negative enrichments. In the case of the former, they were related to metabolism and replication and repair processes. In the case of the latter, it resulted in depletion of diverse metabolic pathways: amino acid metabolism, antimicrobial resistance, signal transduction, cell motility and quorum sensing.

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, positive enrichment was mainly related to nucleotide metabolism, DNA damage and stress response, while negative enrichment was found in diverse metabolic processes, but mainly related to amino acids.
Regarding MF, we found positive enrichment in processes related to energy and nucleic acid metabolism, while the transmembrane transporter activity of organic acids, carboxylic acids and organic anions, in addition to vitamin binding and other enzymatic activities, were depleted.
Regarding CC, positive enrichments were only found in the cytosol and the intracellular protein-containing complex.

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GIL01 strains contrast at 60-mock minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time60"), c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time60")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 10 upregulated and 144 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 678 genes were discarded due to low counts, likely because they are expressed in other contrasts. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)

adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 10, 0.2%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 144, 2.9%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 678, 14%
(mean count < 9)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

Regarding CC, only positive enrichments were found, mainly related to the cytosol, ribosomes and ribonucleoprotein complex components.

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
Boxplots


Code
cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts))



GIL01
In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes.


Code
gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("GIL01_t60-mock"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil01_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil01_annotation %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),
    Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description),
    Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype),
    Gene_ID = factor(
      Gene_ID,
      levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[
        gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID
      ]
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes(
  x = Gene_ID,
  y = CPM_log2,
  text = paste(
    "Gene ID:", Gene_ID,
    ifelse(
      Gene_symbol != "",
      paste0("<br>Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description != "",
      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",
      paste0("<br>Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype),
      ""
    ),
    "<br>Sample:", Sample,
    "<br>Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2)
  )
)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.25,
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.7,
    aes(color = Color)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  ylab("Gene expression") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil01_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








GIL16
In the case of GIL16, the results are similar. Again, the major capsid protein gene (GIL16_18) is the most highly expressed. Although there is greater variation between samples in this case, GIL16-DNT_01 exhibits expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL16_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL16_05) genes.

      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",

  )

gil16_long_cpm <- gil16_long_cpm %>%

    ),

      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),

  )
)) +

    width = 0.25,

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 0 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time60", "StrainGIL16.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 493 upregulated and 512 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 97 genes were discarded due to low counts, likely because they are expressed in other contrasts. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 493, 9.9%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 512, 10%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 97, 1.9%
(mean count < 4)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
EC
The EC classification establishes up to four levels to define enzymes. In this section, the first three were analyzed, achieving significant results for all of them. At the first level, 1 and 7 numbers were positively enriched by 25 oxidorreductases and 14 translocases genes, respectively. In the second, 1.6 and 7.2 numbers were enriched by 6 and 5 upregulated genes, respectively. In the third, the same 6 genes positively enrich the 1.6.5 number, which correspond to NADH-quinone oxidoreductase subunits (GBJ002_02486, GBJ002_02489, GBJ002_02490, GBJ002_02491 and GBJ002_02492) genes and FMN-dependent NADH-azoreductase (GBJ002_04614) gene. Regarding the genes with 7.2 number, they are: calcium-translocating P-type ATPase SERCA-type (GBJ002_01084), magnesium-translocating P-type ATPase (GBJ002_01361), petrobactin import ATP-binding protein FpuC (GBJ002_01590), potassium-transporting ATPase subunit KdpA (GBJ002_03521) and potassium-transporting ATPase subunit KdpB (GBJ002_03522).

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
Boxplots


Code
cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts))



GIL01
In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes.


Code
gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("GIL01_t30"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil01_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil01_annotation %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),
    Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description),
    Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype),
    Gene_ID = factor(
      Gene_ID,
      levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[
        gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID
      ]
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes(
  x = Gene_ID,
  y = CPM_log2,
  text = paste(
    "Gene ID:", Gene_ID,
    ifelse(
      Gene_symbol != "",
      paste0("<br>Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description != "",
      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",
      paste0("<br>Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype),
      ""
    ),
    "<br>Sample:", Sample,
    "<br>Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2)
  )
)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.25,
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.7,
    aes(color = Color)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  ylab("Gene expression") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil16_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

title: Strains GIL01 vs GBJ002 contrast
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL01 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 30 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time30", "StrainGIL01.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 15 upregulated and 48 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 678 genes were discarded due to low counts, likely because they are expressed in other contrasts. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 15, 0.3%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 48, 0.96%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 678, 14%
(mean count < 9)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 10 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time10", "StrainGIL16.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 49 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)

LFC < -0.58 (down) : 49, 0.98%

low counts [2]     : 1355, 27%
(mean count < 22)

[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results

          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
Boxplots


Code
cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts))



GIL01
In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes.


Code
gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("GIL01_t0"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil01_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil01_annotation %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),
    Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description),
    Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype),
    Gene_ID = factor(
      Gene_ID,
      levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[
        gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID
      ]
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes(
  x = Gene_ID,
  y = CPM_log2,
  text = paste(
    "Gene ID:", Gene_ID,
    ifelse(
      Gene_symbol != "",
      paste0("<br>Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description != "",
      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",
      paste0("<br>Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype),
      ""
    ),
    "<br>Sample:", Sample,
    "<br>Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2)
  )
)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.25,
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.7,
    aes(color = Color)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  ylab("Gene expression") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil01_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








GIL16
In the case of GIL16, the results are similar. Again, the major capsid protein gene (GIL16_18) is the most highly expressed. Although there is greater variation between samples in this case, GIL16-DNT_01 exhibits expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL16_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL16_05) genes.

gil16_annotation <- annotation %>%

      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )

gil16_long_cpm <- gil16_long_cpm %>%

    ),

      ""

title: Strains GIL01 vs GBJ002 contrast
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL01 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 0 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, name = "Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, only 1 upregulated gene was obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 1, 0.02%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 0, 0%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 1)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GBJ002 strains contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = c("Strain", "GIL16", "GBJ002"), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value

text:
KEGG
According to KEGG categories, we found several positive enrichments and one negative. The positive enrichments were mainly related to metabolic pathways, but also to translation. The negative enrichment was associated with DNA replication and with GBJ002_00997 (replicative DNA helicase), GBJ002_01520 (DNA primase), GBJ002_05128 (DNA polymerase-3 subunit alpha), GBJ002_05130 (DNA-directed DNA polymerase), GBJ002_05182 (Single-stranded DNA-binding protein) and GBJ002_05251 (AAA family ATPase) genes.

    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("RichFactor", "FoldEnrichment", "zScore"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("pvalue", "p.adjust", "qvalue"), digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we only found positive enrichment in processes related to translation, gene expression and proteins and macromolecules metabolic processes.
Regarding MF, we only observed a high number of enriched functions, mostly related to the binding of RNA-associated molecules. Related processes are also favoured, such as catalytic activity on nucleic acids and ligase activity.
Regarding CC, only positive enrichments were found, mainly related to the cytoplasm, ribosomes and protein complexes.



Code
go_bp_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "BP")
go_bp_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "BP")

go_mf_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "MF")
go_mf_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "MF")

go_cc_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "CC")
go_cc_down <- go_enrichment(down_genes, all_genes, "CC")
