title: GIL16 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
In this section, we load the data necessary to begin the RNAseq analysis: counts and metadata. We also load genome annotation files to visualize the results and the rRNA genes, which are one of the most common sources of noise in RNAseq analyses. Although rRNA gene reads removal was efficient using Bowtie2, we chose to discard these gene counts before normalization.


Code
counts <- readRDS(file = "lytic_cycle_induction_counts.rds")
metadata <- readRDS(file = "lytic_cycle_induction_metadata.rds")
annotation <- readRDS(file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

title: GIL16 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC1
In the case of PC1, we can observe a clear separation into three parts: samples induced at 60 minutes, samples induced at early times and without induction and samples induced at 30 minutes. GIL16_t10_III is grouped with the rest of the 30 minutes samples, as seen in the PCA plot. Regarding the 20 most variable genes, as expected, they correspond to GIL16 genes.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC1), ], labels_row = labels_PC1, legend = FALSE)

title: GIL16 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC2
In the case of PC2, we found less separation between the times. Replicates II and III at times 0 and 10 are grouped in one branch, but replicates I at those times are more closely associated with replicates at time 30. A similar pattern is observed with times 30 and 60, as replicate I at time 30 is associated with 60-mock samples and GIL16_t60_I. In any case, the grouping of samples induced at 60 minutes with others at 10 and 30 minutes seems strange. Although it may seem contradictory, PC1 explains much more variance in gene expression than PC2 and 20 genes might prove insufficient to properly cluster samples in this component. To clarify these differences, we will examine the clustering between samples in the heatmap.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC2), ], labels_row = labels_PC2, legend = FALSE)

title: GBJ002 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
In this section, we load the data necessary to begin the RNAseq analysis: counts and metadata. We also load genome annotation files to visualize the results.


Code
counts <- readRDS(file = "lytic_cycle_induction_counts.rds")
metadata <- readRDS(file = "lytic_cycle_induction_metadata.rds")
annotation <- readRDS(file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

title: GBJ002 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC1
In the case of PC1, we see that all samples cluster according to their time, except for replicate III at minute 10, which is more similar to those at time 0. In any case, the most different samples are those at time 60. Regarding the genes, we see a clear difference in the genes of the prophage region: expression is much higher at minute 60 induced samples than in the other groups.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC1), ], labels_row = labels_PC1, legend = FALSE)

title: GBJ002 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC2
In the case of PC2, all samples cluster according to their time, but this time there is a separation between the 60 and 60-mock samples from the rest, as seen in PCA. The greatest difference in gene expression is found in the genes GBJ002_01175 to GBJ002_01179, which encode two phosphoesterases, two SR1 sRNA and one SR1 protein.
According to RFAM, SR1 RNA is a small RNA (sRNA) produced by species of Bacillus and closely related bacteria whose expression is regulated by CcpA and CcpN. It is a dual-function RNA which acts both as a protein-coding RNA and as a regulatory sRNA:

SR1 RNA is involved in the regulation of arginine catabolism. SR1 RNA binds to complementary stretches of ahrC mRNA (also known as argR) and inhibits translation. AhrC endodes an arginine repressor protein which represses synthesis of arginine biosynthetic enzymes and activates arginine catabolic enzymes via regulation of the rocABC and rocDEF operons.
In addition to acting as a sRNA, SR1 also encodes a small peptide, SR1P. SR1P binds to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GapA) and stabilises the gapA operon mRNAs.



Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC2), ], labels_row = labels_PC2, legend = FALSE)

title: Lysogeny analysis
text:
Raw counts were obtained by preprocessing featureCounts results. Specifically, they were obtained by summing counts of paired reads plus half the sum of the counts of unpaired reads:
\[
Counts = \sum \text{paired reads counts} + \frac{\sum \text{unpaired reads counts}}{2}
\]
Once the counts are obtained, we upload them along with the metadata.


Code
counts <- read.csv("lysogeny_counts.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

metadata <- read.csv("lysogeny_metadata.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
metadata$Strain <- factor(metadata$Strain, levels = c("GBJ002", "GIL01", "GIL16"))

annotation <- readRDS(file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

title: Lysogeny analysis
text:
PC1
In the case of PC1, we can see two main branches: the left branch, with all samples of GIL16, and the right branch, with GBJ002 and GIL01 samples. Regarding the genes, we see three clearly defined regions. The upper region contains GBJ002_04202 (flagellar basal body rod protein) and GBJ002_04203 (phage shock protein A) genes, which are very poorly expressed in the GBJ002 samples, at an intermediate level in GIL01 and at a high level in GIL16. The intermediate region contains pBtic235 genes, which are highly expressed in GBJ002, less in GIL01 and poorly expressed in GIL16. Finally, we find prophage region genes and two pBtic235 genes, which exhibit a similar pattern to the previous one, although in this case they are more repressed in samples containing phages. Overall, this heatmap suggests that GIL01 in lisogeny causes milder transcriptional changes than GIL16.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC1), ], labels_row = labels_PC1, legend = FALSE)

title: Lysogeny analysis
text:
PC2
In the case of PC2, we can see two main branches: the left branch, with all samples of GBJ002, and the right branch, with GIL01 and GIL16 samples. Regarding the genes, we see three main regions. The upper region contains low expressed chromosomal genes in the three conditions. The intermediate region contains chromosomal genes, including some from the prophage region, that are repressed in samples containing phages. Finally, we found a region containing genes that are highly expressed in the presence of GIL01 and less expressed under other conditions. The following genes are particularly interesting because they are located close to each other: GBJ002_02402 (chitin disaccharide deacetylase), GBJ002_02403 (6-phospho-beta-glucosidase), GBJ002_02404 (lichenan-specific phosphotransferase enzyme IIA component), GBJ002_02405 (PTS cellobiose transporter subunit IIC) and GBJ002_02406 (lichenan-specific phosphotransferase enzyme IIB component). The other two genes are located downstream of the previous ones and the difference is not as noticeable, although they are worth highlighting: GBJ002_03363 (bacillolysin) and GBJ002_04981 (cation efflux protein cytoplasmic domain-containing protein).


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC2), ], labels_row = labels_PC2, legend = FALSE)

title: Lysogeny analysis
section: Explained variance per gene and factor
text:
Explained variance per gene and factor
First, we compared the proportion of gene expression variance explained by Strain and by Replicate as a proxy for batch effects. To ensure comparability across all lysogeny samples, this analysis was restricted to the gene set shared across conditions, corresponding to genes from GBJ002 (including both chromosomal genes and those located on pBtic235).
At the gene level, Strain accounted for a substantially larger fraction of expression variability than Replicate. Specifically, the median proportion of variance explained by the Strain was 74.68%, whereas Replicate accounted for a median of 5.08% across genes. Thus, biological effects explained approximately fifteen times more variance than batch effects at the gene level.
These results indicate that thre is no batch effect associated with Replicate and that the primary source of variability in the data is driven by Strain, supporting the suitability of the dataset for downstream differential expression analyses, not being necessary to include Replicate as a covariate.


Code
metadata <- metadata %>%
  mutate(Replicate = factor(sub(".*_", "", Sample)))

dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts,
  colData   = metadata,
  design    = ~ Replicate + Strain
)

dds <- DESeq(dds)
dds <- dds[!grepl("^GIL", rownames(dds)), ]

expr_mat <- assay(vst(dds, blind = FALSE))

var_explained <- apply(expr_mat, 1, function(gene_expr) {
  model <- aov(gene_expr ~ Strain + Replicate, data = metadata)
  ss <- summary(model)[[1]][["Sum Sq"]]
  total <- sum(ss)
  c(
    Strain     = ss[1] / total,
    Replicate = ss[2] / total
  )
})

var_explained <- t(var_explained) %>% as.data.frame()

var_long <- var_explained %>%
  mutate(Gene = rownames(var_explained)) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c("Strain", "Replicate"),
    names_to = "Factor",
    values_to = "VarianceExplained"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
      VariancePct = VarianceExplained * 100,
      hover_text = paste0(
        "Gene: ", Gene,
        "<br>Factor: ", Factor,
        "<br>Explained variance (%): ", round(VariancePct, 2)
      )
    )

p <- ggplot(
  var_long,
  aes(x = Factor, y = VariancePct, fill = Factor, text = hover_text)
) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.25, alpha = 0.3, size = 0.5) +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylab("Explained variance (%)") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")

title: Lysogeny analysis
text:
Permutational Multivariate Analysis of Variance (PERMANOVA)
To assess batch effects at the transcriptome-wide level, we used permutational multivariate analysis of variance (PERMANOVA) as implemented in the adonis2 function. This method evaluates whether predefined factors explain significant differences in multivariate expression profiles by partitioning the total variance of a distance matrix (here, based on variance-stabilized expression values) among explanatory variables. Statistical significance is assessed via permutations, making the test robust to deviations from normality and suitable for high-dimensional RNA‑seq data. Unlike gene‑wise models, PERMANOVA evaluates effects on the global expression structure, providing an estimate of the proportion of total variance (R²) attributable to each factor.

Global batch test
We first performed a global PERMANOVA including Strain as the main biological factor and Replicate as a potential batch effect. In this model, biological structure clearly dominated the organization of the transcriptomic space. Strain explained the vast majority of the variance (R² = 84.35%, p = 0.002), indicating strong transcriptional differences between the strains. In contrast, Replicate accounted for only a small fraction of the total variation (R² = 4.86%) and did not show statistical significance (p = 0.584). This indicates that, while replicate-to-replicate variability exists, it does not systematically structure the expression profiles nor introduce detectable batch effects at the global level.
Overall, the PERMANOVA demonstrates that transcriptomic variation is overwhelmingly driven by Strain, and that the variability associated with Replicate is minor, non‑significant and does not dominate the expression landscape.


Code
adonis_result <- adonis2(
  t(expr_mat) ~ Strain + Replicate,
  data = metadata,
  permutations = 9999,
  by = "terms"
)

datatable(adonis_result, colnames = c("Term", "DF", "SS", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("SumOfSqs", "F", "Pr(>F)"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2)








Batch effect within biological strata
To further evaluate whether the effect of Replicate persists once biological structure is fully controlled, we performed a PERMANOVA in which permutations were restricted within Strain. Under this design, samples were only compared to others from the same strain, ensuring that any residual structure attributable to Replicate could be detected independently of differences between strains. This analysis specifically tests whether Replicate induces systematic within‑strain shifts in expression profiles.
Under this restricted model, Replicate explained only a very small fraction of the total transcriptomic variance (R² = 4.86%) and did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.611). The modest effect size, combined with a clearly non‑significant p‑value, indicates that Replicate does not introduce consistent transcriptomic differences among biologically matched samples.
Importantly, the proportion of variance attributed to Replicate in the within‑strain model was nearly identical to that observed in the global PERMANOVA. However, in both analyses Replicate remained non‑significant. This indicates that the small amount of variance associated with Replicate does not reflect a consistent or systematic batch effect, but rather random variation that does not meaningfully structure the transcriptomic space.
Together, these results indicate that Replicate does not introduce a reproducible batch effect in the dataset. Its contribution to overall variation is minimal, unstable and statistically indistinguishable from random noise once biological differences between strains are controlled.


Code
adonis_batch_within <- adonis2(
  t(expr_mat) ~ Replicate,
  data = metadata,
  permutations = 9999,
  strata = metadata$Strain
)

datatable(adonis_batch_within, colnames = c("Term", "DF", "SS", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("SumOfSqs", "F", "Pr(>F)"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2)

title: Bacillus thuringiensis–Betatectivirus dual RNAseq analysis
text:
On this page, we will show the general RNAseq analysis using DESeq2 of Bacillus thuringiensis GBJ002 strain vs lysogens of GIL01 and GIL16 tectiviruses. The different sections show the code used to obtain the results shown.

Load reads processing data
Once the raw reads were obtained, trimming was performed using fastp. In this processing step, low-quality sequences and adapters were removed. Next, genomic sequences of Phi-X174, used as a control in Illumina sequences, and human sequences, which may have been present due to sample handling, were removed using Bowtie2. Then, potential contaminating microorganisms were checked using MetaPhlAn. The species Cutibacterium acnes, Paracoccus yeei and Kocuria carniphila were found in some reads and were subsequently removed using Bowtie2 with the reference genomes of these species. Finally, using Bowtie2 again and the SILVA database as a reference, rRNA sequences were removed. After all processing steps, in almost all samples, the filtered reads represented more than 90% of the original reads. There were only two exceptions: GIL01_t30_I, because it had a small contamination by K. carniphila, and GIL16_t10_I, which had a higher proportion of rRNA reads. To simplify visualization, the reads removed in the decontamination steps are represented merged in the Contamination category.


Code
rp_data <- read.csv("rp_data.tsv", header = TRUE, sep = "\t", check.names = FALSE)

rp_data <- rp_data %>%
  mutate(Contamination = rowSums(across(3:7))) %>%
  dplyr::select(1, 2, Contamination, 8:ncol(rp_data))

steps <- colnames(rp_data)[2:5]

rp_data_long <- rp_data %>%
  mutate(parts = str_split(Sample, "_")) %>%
  mutate(
    Organism = map_chr(parts, 1),
    Group = map_chr(parts, ~ if (length(.) == 3) .[2] else "Lysogeny"),
    Replicate = map_chr(parts, ~ if (length(.) == 3) .[3] else .[2])
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(-parts) %>%
  relocate(Organism, Group, Replicate, .after = Sample) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = all_of(steps),
    names_to = "Processing_step",
    values_to = "Reads_step"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Processing_step = factor(Processing_step, levels = steps),
    Percentage = Reads_step / Total,
    PercentagePct = Percentage * 100,
    hover_text = paste0(
      "Sample: ", Sample,
      "<br>Processing step: ", Processing_step,
      "<br>Reads (%): ", sprintf("%.2f", PercentagePct),
      "<br>Reads: ", comma(Reads_step),
      "<br>Total reads: ", comma(Total)
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(
  rp_data_long,
  aes(
    x = Group,
    y = PercentagePct,
    color = Processing_step
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    aes(text = hover_text),
    size = 3,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Organism, nrow = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    y = "Reads (%)",
    x = NULL,
    color = "Processing step"
  ) +
 scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Trimming" = "red",
    "Contamination" = "salmon1",
    "rRNA" = "thistle",
    "Filtered" = "steelblue1"
 )) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








Load alignment data
Prior to obtaining the raw gene counts, sequencing reads were first mapped against genomes using Bowtie2 in order to assess mapping quality and identify potential outlier samples. Mapping statistics were subsequently extracted using Samtools. The interactive plot shown below summarizes the percentage of mapped reads for each sample group. By hovering over each data point, detailed information is displayed, including the sample identifier, the proportion of mapped reads (both as a percentage and as absolute counts), and the total number of reads per sample.


Code
mapped_reads <- read.csv("mapped_reads.tsv", header = TRUE, sep = "\t")

df_I <- mapped_reads %>%
  filter(grepl("_I_", Sample)) %>%
  separate(Sample, into = c("Sample", "Reads"), sep = "_I_", extra = "merge") %>%
  mutate(Sample = paste0(Sample, "_I"))

df_II <- mapped_reads %>%
  filter(grepl("_II_", Sample)) %>%
  separate(Sample, into = c("Sample", "Reads"), sep = "_II_", extra = "merge") %>%
  mutate(Sample = paste0(Sample, "_II"))

df_III <- mapped_reads %>%
  filter(grepl("_III_", Sample)) %>%
  separate(Sample, into = c("Sample", "Reads"), sep = "_III_", extra = "merge") %>%
  mutate(Sample = paste0(Sample, "_III"))

mapped_reads_new <- bind_rows(df_I, df_II, df_III)

mapped_reads_new <- mapped_reads_new %>%
  arrange(Sample, Reads)

unaligned_cols <- grep("_unaligned$", colnames(mapped_reads_new), value = TRUE)

mapped_reads_new <- mapped_reads_new %>%
  mutate(Unaligned = Unaligned + rowSums(across(all_of(unaligned_cols)))) %>%
  dplyr::select(-all_of(unaligned_cols))

mapped_reads_summary <- mapped_reads_new %>%
  dplyr::select(-Reads) %>%
  group_by(Sample) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), sum, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  dplyr::select(Sample, chromosome, pBtic235, GIL01, GIL16, Unaligned) %>%
  mutate(Reads = rowSums(across(where(is.numeric))))

alignment_data <- mapped_reads_summary %>%
  dplyr::select(-Reads) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ .x / rowSums(across(where(is.numeric))))) %>%
  mutate(
    parts = str_split(Sample, "_")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Organism = map_chr(parts, 1),
    Group = map_chr(parts, ~ if(length(.) == 3) .[2] else "Lysogeny"),
    Replicate = map_chr(parts, ~ if(length(.) == 3) .[3] else .[2])
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(-parts) %>%
  relocate(Organism, Group, Replicate, .after = Sample) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(chromosome, pBtic235, GIL01, GIL16, Unaligned),
    names_to = "Region",
    values_to = "Percentage"
  ) %>%
  mutate(Region = factor(Region, levels = c("chromosome", "pBtic235", "GIL01", "GIL16", "Unaligned"))) %>%
  filter(Percentage != 0) %>%
  left_join(mapped_reads_summary, by = "Sample") %>%
  mutate(
    PercentagePct = Percentage * 100,
    RegionReads = case_when(
      Region == "chromosome" ~ chromosome,
      Region == "pBtic235"   ~ pBtic235,
      Region == "GIL01"      ~ GIL01,
      Region == "GIL16"      ~ GIL16,
      Region == "Unaligned"  ~ Unaligned,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    ),
    hover_text = paste0(
      "Sample: ", Sample,
      "<br>Mapped reads (%): ", sprintf("%.2f", PercentagePct),
      "<br>Mapped reads: ", comma(RegionReads),
      "<br>Total reads: ", comma(Reads)
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(
  alignment_data,
  aes(
    x = Group,
    y = PercentagePct,
    color = Region
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    aes(text = hover_text),
    size = 3,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Organism, nrow = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    y = "Mapped reads (%)",
    x = NULL,
    color = "Region"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "chromosome" = "brown1",
    "pBtic235"   = "tan1",
    "GIL01"      = "blue1",
    "GIL16"      = "green1",
    "Unaligned"  = "grey50"
  )) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








Load counts
Raw counts were obtained by preprocessing featureCounts results. Specifically, they were obtained by summing counts of paired reads plus half the sum of the counts of unpaired reads:
\[
Counts = \sum \text{paired reads counts} + \frac{\sum \text{unpaired reads counts}}{2}
\]
Once the counts were obtained, we upload them along with annotated rRNA genes. rRNA genes are one of the most common sources of noise in RNAseq analyses. Although rRNA gene reads removal was efficient using Bowtie2, we chose to discard these gene counts before normalization. In the following heatmap, we can see the samples that had a higher percentage of rRNA counts. As we can see, none of the samples after removal by Bowtie2 had more than 0.1% rRNA counts, although we removed the rRNA genes so that there would be no artifacts in the subsequent results.


Code
counts <- read.csv("counts.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)
rrna_genes <- rownames(read.csv("rrna.tsv", sep = "\t", header = FALSE, row.names = 1))
rrna_counts <- counts[rownames(counts) %in% rrna_genes, ]
total_rrna_counts <- colSums(rrna_counts)
total_counts <- colSums(counts)
rrna_data <- unique(alignment_data[, 1:4])

rrna_df <- rrna_data %>%
  mutate(
    ratio = (total_rrna_counts[Sample]/total_counts[Sample]) * 100,
    rrna_counts = total_rrna_counts[Sample],
    total_counts = total_counts[Sample],
    hover_text = paste0(
      "Replicate: ", Replicate,
      "<br>rRNA counts (%): ", sprintf("%.4f", ratio),
      "<br>rRNA counts: ", scales::comma(rrna_counts),
      "<br>Total counts: ", scales::comma(total_counts)
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(
  rrna_df,
  aes(
    x = Group,
    y = ratio,
    color = Replicate
  )
) +
  geom_point(
    aes(text = hover_text),
    size = 3,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.3)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Organism, nrow = 3, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    y = "rRNA counts (%)",
    x = NULL,
    color = "Replicate"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








Genomes annotation
This section presents the annotation of bacterial and phage genomes performed using Bakta and Pharokka, respectively. This annotation was supplemented by the identification of prophage regions (reviewed using CheckV), biosynthetic gene clusters (antiSMASH and BAGEL4), defense systems (PADLOC and DefenseFinder), AMR genes (ResFinder, AMRFinderPlus and RGI), virulence factors (AMRFinderPlus and VFDB) and the coding probability of de novo transcripts (Popcorn).


Code
saf <- read.csv("GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.saf", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
bakta <- read.csv("annotation.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
cog_annotation <- read.csv("cog_annotation.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
additional_annotation <- read.csv("additional_annotation.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
tectivirus_annotation <- read.csv("tectivirus_annotation.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)

annotation <- saf %>%
  rename(Gene_ID = GeneID, Element = Chr) %>%
  left_join(
    bakta %>% dplyr::select(`Locus.Tag`, Gene, Product),
    by = c("Gene_ID" = "Locus.Tag")
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    cog_annotation %>% dplyr::select(ID, COG),
    by = c("Gene_ID" = "ID")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Gene_symbol = Gene,
    Description = Product,
    Classification = COG,
    Association = NA_character_,
    Type = NA_character_,
    Subtype = NA_character_,
    Source = NA_character_
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(-Gene, -Product, -COG)

annotation_both <- additional_annotation %>% filter(Strand == "Both")

for (i in seq_len(nrow(annotation_both))) {
  row <- annotation_both[i, ]
  idx <- which(
    annotation$Element == row$Element &
    annotation$End >= row$Start &
    annotation$Start <= row$End
  )
  if (length(idx) > 0) {
    for (j in idx) {
      g_start <- annotation$Start[j]
      g_end <- annotation$End[j]
      if (g_start >= row$Start & g_end <= row$End) {
        tag_association <- row$Association
      } else {
        tag_association <- paste0(row$Association, "*")
      }
      annotation$Association[j] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Association[j], tag_association)
      annotation$Type[j] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Type[j], row$Type)
      annotation$Subtype[j] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Subtype[j], row$Subtype)
      annotation$Source[j] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Source[j], row$Source)
    }
  }
}

annotation_strand <- additional_annotation %>% filter(Strand %in% c("+", "-"))

for (i in seq_len(nrow(annotation_strand))) {
  row <- annotation_strand[i, ]
  idx <- which(
    annotation$Element == row$Element &
    annotation$Start == row$Start &
    annotation$End == row$End &
    annotation$Strand == row$Strand
  )
  if (length(idx) > 0) {
    annotation$Association[idx] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Association[idx], row$Association)
    annotation$Type[idx] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Type[idx], row$Type)
    annotation$Subtype[idx] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Subtype[idx], row$Subtype)
    annotation$Source[idx] <- concat_or_replace(annotation$Source[idx], row$Source)
  }
}

idx <- match(annotation$Gene_ID, tectivirus_annotation$ID)

annotation$Description <- dplyr::coalesce(
  tectivirus_annotation$Description[idx],
  annotation$Description
)

annotation$Gene_symbol <- dplyr::coalesce(
  tectivirus_annotation$Gene_symbol[idx],
  annotation$Gene_symbol
)

annotation$Classification <- dplyr::coalesce(
  as.character(tectivirus_annotation$Gene_number[idx]),
  annotation$Classification
)

annotation$Element <- factor(annotation$Element, levels = c("chromosome", "pBtic235", "GIL01", "GIL16"))
annotation$Strand <- factor(annotation$Strand, levels = c("+", "-"))

datatable(annotation, colnames = c("Gene ID", "Element", "Start", "End", "Strand",
                                   "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category / Homologue", "Association", "Type",
                                   "Subtype", "Source"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE))








Save results


Code
saveRDS(annotation, file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

title: Lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
Raw counts were obtained by preprocessing featureCounts results. Specifically, they were obtained by summing counts of paired reads plus half the sum of the counts of unpaired reads:
\[
Counts = \sum \text{paired reads counts} + \frac{\sum \text{unpaired reads counts}}{2}
\]
Once the counts are obtained, we upload them along with the metadata.


Code
counts <- read.csv("lytic_cycle_induction_counts.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE, row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE)

metadata <- read.csv("lytic_cycle_induction_metadata.tsv", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)
metadata[] <- lapply(metadata, function(x) if (is.character(x)) gsub("-", "_", x) else x)
metadata$Strain <- as.factor(metadata$Strain)
metadata$Time <- as.factor(metadata$Time)
metadata$MMC <- as.factor(metadata$MMC)
metadata$Replicate <- as.factor(metadata$Replicate)

annotation <- readRDS(file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

title: Lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC1
In the case of PC1, we can see two main branches: the left branch, with all samples induced at 60 minutes, and the right branch, with the rest. In the right branch, the separation of samples according to the strain or the time intervals is not as clear. Regarding the genes, we see two clearly defined regions. In the upper region are 9 genes from the prophage region, which are repressed in most samples, but their expression increases 60 minutes after induction with MMC. In the lower region, we find 11 genes belonging to pBtic235. In this case, expression appears weaker in some samples from minute 0 or without induction (60-mock) and increases at 60 minutes. Furthermore, it is interesting to note the presence of a de novo transcript (DNT) among the most variable genes, excluding phage genes.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC1), ], labels_row = labels_PC1, legend = FALSE)

title: Lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC2
In the case of PC2, there appears to be some separation between the samples induced at early time points and the rest. Regarding the genes, we observe the following differences from top to bottom:

The highest expression of GBJ002_03168 (arginine deiminase), GBJ002_03169 (ornithine carbamoyltransferase), GBJ002_03170 (arginine-ornithine antiporter) and GBJ002_03171 (carbamate kinase) genes is found in late time points, regardless of MMC induction.
GBJ002_03392 (PTS trehalose transporter subunit IIBC) and GBJ002_03393 (alpha,alpha-phosphotrehalase) genes, on the other hand, are more highly expressed in early and intermediate time points than in late time points.
GBJ002_02405 (PTS cellobiose transporter subunit IIC), GBJ002_03173 (ABC transporter permease) and GBJ002_04467 (group-specific protein) genes are similar to the first four, although their expression is weaker in this case.
GBJ002_00877 (PTS system fructose-specific EIIABC component), GBJ002_00878 (1-phosphofructokinase) and GBJ002_00879 (HTH deoR-type domain-containing protein) genes are more highly expressed in early time points, with their expression being weakest in the uninduced samples.
GBJ002_00714 (aldehyde dehydrogenase DhaS), GBJ002_01842 (argininosuccinate lyase), GBJ002_01843 (argininosuccinate synthase), GBJ002_02402 (chitin disaccharide deacetylase) and GBJ002_02406 (lichenan-specific phosphotransferase enzyme IIB component) genes have low expression in early time points and intermediate expression in later time points.
GBJ002_00049 (foldase protein PrsA 4), GBJ002_00160 (pyrroline-5-carboxylate reductase) and GBJ002_05018 (transcriptional regulator, ArsR family) genes have the inverse expression of the previous five.

These results seem to suggest metabolic changes and that certain genes are co-expressed with others, regardless of their location on the chromosome. Therefore, it would be interesting to investigate the coexpression of genes throughout MMC induction.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC2), ], labels_row = labels_PC2, legend = FALSE)

title: Lytic cycle induction analysis
section: Explained variance per gene and factor
text:
Explained variance per gene and factor
First, we compared the proportion of gene expression variance explained by the experimental design (i.e., biological factors corresponding to Strain, Time and MMC) and by Replicate as a proxy for batch effects. To ensure comparability across all lytic cycle inducted samples, this analysis was restricted to the gene set shared across conditions, corresponding to genes from GBJ002 (including both chromosomal genes and those located on pBtic235).
At the gene level, biological factors accounted for a substantially larger fraction of expression variability than Replicate. Specifically, the median proportion of variance explained by the experimental structure was 70.92%, whereas Replicate accounted for a median of 2.06% across genes.
These results indicate that, although a batch effect associated with Replicate is present and detectable, it does not dominate the expression signal. Instead, the primary source of variability in the data is driven by the intended experimental factors, supporting the suitability of the dataset for downstream differential expression analyses when Replicate is appropriately included as a covariate.


Code
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts,
  colData   = metadata,
  design    = ~ Replicate + Strain * Time + Strain * MMC
)

dds <- DESeq(dds)
dds <- dds[!grepl("^GIL", rownames(dds)), ]

expr_mat <- assay(vst(dds, blind = FALSE))

var_explained <- apply(expr_mat, 1, function(gene_expr) {
  
  model <- aov(
    gene_expr ~ Strain * Time + Strain * MMC + Replicate,
    data = metadata
  )
  
  tab <- summary(model)[[1]]
  
  ss <- tab[, "Sum Sq"]
  names(ss) <- trimws(rownames(tab))
  
  total <- sum(ss, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  c(
    Strain_Time_MMC = unname(
      sum(
        ss[c("Strain", "Time", "MMC", "Strain:Time", "Strain:MMC")],
        na.rm = TRUE
      ) / total
    ),
    Replicate = unname(
      ss["Replicate"] / total
    )
  )
})

var_explained <- t(var_explained) %>% as.data.frame()

var_long <- var_explained %>%
  mutate(Gene = rownames(var_explained)) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c("Strain_Time_MMC", "Replicate"),
    names_to = "Factor",
    values_to = "VarianceExplained"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    VariancePct = VarianceExplained * 100,
    hover_text = paste0(
      "Gene: ", Gene,
      "<br>Factor: ", Factor,
      "<br>Explained variance (%): ", round(VariancePct, 2)
    )
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Factor = recode(
      Factor,
      Strain_Time_MMC = "Biological factors",
      Replicate = "Replicate"
    ),
    Factor = factor(
      Factor,
      levels = c("Biological factors", "Replicate")
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(
  var_long,
  aes(x = Factor, y = VariancePct, fill = Factor, text = hover_text)
) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.25, alpha = 0.3, size = 0.5) +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylab("Explained variance (%)") +
  xlab(NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")

title: Lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
Permutational Multivariate Analysis of Variance (PERMANOVA)
To assess batch effects at the transcriptome-wide level, we used permutational multivariate analysis of variance (PERMANOVA) as implemented in the adonis2 function. This method evaluates whether predefined factors explain significant differences in multivariate expression profiles by partitioning the total variance of a distance matrix (here, based on variance-stabilized expression values) among explanatory variables. Statistical significance is assessed via permutations, making the test robust to deviations from normality and suitable for high-dimensional RNA‑seq data. Unlike gene‑wise models, PERMANOVA evaluates effects on the global expression structure, providing an estimate of the proportion of total variance (R²) attributable to each factor.

Global batch test
We first performed a global PERMANOVA including the main biological factors and its interactions together with Replicate as a potential batch effect. In this model, biological factors explained the majority of the transcriptomic variation. Time was the dominant source of variability (R² = 25.60%, p < 0.001), followed by MMC (R² = 21.41%, p < 0.001) and Strain (R² = 11.74%, p < 0.001). Replicate accounted for a much smaller fraction of the total variance (R² = 2.77%) and showed a marginal effect (p = 0.0584). This suggests that, although Replicate introduces some variability in the data, its contribution is minor compared to the intended biological structure of the experiment.
Overall, the global PERMANOVA indicates that transcriptomic variation is primarily driven by biological factors, while the batch effect associated with Replicate is limited in magnitude and does not dominate the expression space.


Code
adonis_result <- adonis2(
  t(expr_mat) ~ Strain * Time + Strain * MMC + Replicate,
  data = metadata,
  permutations = 9999,
  by = "terms"
)

datatable(adonis_result, colnames = c("Term", "DF", "SS", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("SumOfSqs", "F"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2)








Batch effect within biological strata
To further evaluate whether the effect of Replicate persists once biological variability is fully controlled, we performed a PERMANOVA restricted to biologically equivalent samples. In this analysis, permutations were constrained within Strain × Time × MMC strata, such that samples were only compared against others sharing the same strain, time point and induction condition. This approach specifically tests whether Replicate introduces systematic differences beyond the biological structure of the experiment.
Under this restricted model, Replicate explained a small but statistically significant fraction of the total transcriptomic variance (R² = 2.77%, p = 0.003). Although the effect size remained modest, the significant result indicates that Replicate consistently affects expression profiles when comparisons are limited to biologically matched samples.
Importantly, the proportion of variance explained by Replicate was identical to that observed in the global model, indicating that the magnitude of the batch effect is stable and not driven by confounding with strain or time. The increased statistical significance in the stratified analysis reflects the removal of biological heterogeneity, which increases sensitivity to detect technical effects.
Together, these results demonstrate that Replicate introduces a reproducible batch effect across samples. While this effect is small relative to biological factors, it is statistically robust and should therefore be accounted for in downstream analyses.


Code
adonis_batch_within <- adonis2(
  t(expr_mat) ~ Replicate,
  data = metadata,
  permutations = 9999,
  strata = interaction(metadata$Strain, metadata$Time, metadata$MMC)
)

datatable(adonis_batch_within, colnames = c("Term", "DF", "SS", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("SumOfSqs", "F"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2)








Interaction between strain and batch effect
To assess whether the effect of Replicate differs between strains, we extended the PERMANOVA model to include an interaction term between Strain and Replicate while accounting for the experimental factors Time and MMC. Marginal tests were used to evaluate each term independently of the order in which factors enter the model.
In this model, the interaction between Strain and Time remained highly significant (R² = 13.02%, p < 0.001), confirming that temporal expression dynamics differ across strains and reinforcing the biological relevance of modeling this interaction in downstream analyses.
In addition, the interaction between Strain and MMC was also highly significant (R² = 6.42%, p < 0.001), indicating that the transcriptional response to MMC differs among strains. This result demonstrates that strain-specific induction responses contribute substantially to the overall structure of the transcriptomic data and supports the inclusion of strain-dependent MMC effects in the statistical models.
In contrast, the interaction between Strain and Replicate explained a small fraction of the total variance (R² = 4.60%) and showed only a marginal effect (p = 0.0581). This indicates limited evidence that the batch effect associated with Replicate differs systematically between strains. While minor strain‑specific differences in batch response cannot be completely excluded, the overall magnitude of this interaction is small compared to both the main biological effects and the Strain × Time interaction.
Together, these results suggest that Replicate introduces a broadly similar effect across strains rather than strong strain-specific batch effects. Consequently, modeling Replicate as an additive covariate is sufficient to account for batch effects, whereas the significant Strain × Time and Strain × MMC interactions should be explicitly considered when modeling gene expression differences across experimental conditions.


Code
adonis_inter <- adonis2(
  t(expr_mat) ~ Strain * Time + Strain * MMC + Replicate + Strain:Replicate,
  data = metadata,
  permutations = 9999,
  by = "margin"
)

datatable(adonis_inter, colnames = c("Term", "DF", "SS", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("SumOfSqs", "F"), digits = 3) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2)








Batch effect assessed separately for each strain
To further evaluate whether the batch effect associated with Replicate differs across strains, we performed PERMANOVA analyses independently for each strain. In these tests, only samples belonging to a given strain were considered and permutations were restricted within Time × MMC strata to control for both temporal and induction-related effects. This approach allows the assessment of batch effects within each strain while accounting for the major biological sources of expression variation.
Across all three strains, Replicate explained a similar proportion of transcriptomic variance, with R² values of 8.21% for GBJ002, 8.12% for GIL01 and 8.59% for GIL16. In all cases, the effect of Replicate was statistically significant (p < 0.05), indicating that it consistently contributes to expression variability within each strain.
Importantly, both the magnitude and direction of the batch effect were highly comparable across strains. No strain exhibited a disproportionately large batch contribution, and differences in R² values were minimal. These results indicate that the batch effect associated with Replicate is reproducible but homogeneous across strains, rather than being driven by strain-specific technical artifacts.
Together with the interaction analysis, these findings support the conclusion that Replicate introduces a small but consistent batch effect that affects all strains similarly. Consequently, modeling Replicate as a shared covariate across strains is sufficient to account for batch effects in downstream analyses.


Code
strain_levels <- unique(metadata$Strain)

res_list <- lapply(strain_levels, function(strain) {

  idx <- metadata$Strain == strain

  md_sub <- droplevels(metadata[idx, ])
  ex_sub <- expr_mat[, idx, drop = FALSE]

  md_sub$Time_MMC <- interaction(
    md_sub$Time,
    md_sub$MMC,
    drop = TRUE
  )

  ad2 <- adonis2(
    t(ex_sub) ~ Replicate,
    data = md_sub,
    permutations = 9999,
    strata = md_sub$Time_MMC
  )

  data.frame(
    Strain = strain,
    Df = ad2$Df[1],
    R2 = ad2$R2[1],
    F = ad2$F[1],
    p = ad2$`Pr(>F)`[1]
  )
})

batch_per_strain <- do.call(rbind, res_list)

datatable(batch_per_strain, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Strain", "DF", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "F", digits = 3) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "p", digits = 3)








Batch effect assessment including phage‑specific gene sets
Because phages (GIL01 and GIL16) contain genes that are not shared, we additionally evaluated the batch effect using strain-specific gene sets. For this purpose, independent DESeq2 objects were constructed for each strain, allowing the assessment of batch effects on the full transcriptome of each strain and avoiding potential underestimation of technical variability caused by restricting the analysis to shared genes.
For each strain-specific dataset, variance-stabilized expression values were analyzed using PERMANOVA, testing the effect of Replicate while restricting permutations within Time × MMC strata to control for both temporal and induction-related effects. Under this framework, Replicate explained a comparable fraction of transcriptomic variance across all strains, with R² values of 8.11% for GBJ002, 7.76% for GIL01 and 8.19% for GIL16. In all cases, the effect of Replicate was statistically significant (p < 0.05).
These results closely mirror those obtained using the shared gene set and confirm that the batch effect associated with Replicate is not driven by the exclusion of strain-specific genes. Instead, Replicate consistently contributes a small but reproducible proportion of expression variability across the full gene complement of each strain.
Taken together, the consistency of effect sizes across strains and gene sets indicates that the batch effect is homogeneous and moderate in magnitude. This supports the use of a common modeling strategy in downstream analyses, whereby Replicate is included as an additive covariate to control for batch effects without applying explicit batch correction.


Code
make_dds_by_strain <- function(strain, counts, metadata) {

  idx_samples <- metadata$Strain == strain
  md_sub <- droplevels(metadata[idx_samples, ])
  counts_sub <- counts[, idx_samples, drop = FALSE]

  if (strain == "GBJ002") {
    counts_sub <- counts_sub[!grepl("^GIL", rownames(counts_sub)), ]
  }

  if (strain == "GIL01") {
    counts_sub <- counts_sub[!grepl("^GIL16", rownames(counts_sub)), ]
  }

  if (strain == "GIL16") {
    counts_sub <- counts_sub[!grepl("^GIL01", rownames(counts_sub)), ]
  }

  DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
    countData = counts_sub,
    colData   = md_sub,
    design    = ~ Replicate + Time + MMC
  )
}

strains <- levels(metadata$Strain)

dds_list <- lapply(strains, function(strain) {
  make_dds_by_strain(
    strain = strain,
    counts = counts,
    metadata = metadata
  )
})

names(dds_list) <- strains

adonis_by_strain <- lapply(names(dds_list), function(strain) {

  dds <- DESeq(dds_list[[strain]])
  expr <- assay(vst(dds, blind = FALSE))

  ad2 <- adonis2(
    t(expr) ~ Replicate,
    data = as.data.frame(colData(dds)),
    permutations = 9999,
    strata = interaction(colData(dds)$Time, colData(dds)$MMC, drop = TRUE)
  )

  data.frame(
    Strain = strain,
    R2 = ad2$R2[1],
    F = ad2$F[1],
    p = ad2$`Pr(>F)`[1]
  )
})

adonis_by_strain <- do.call(rbind, adonis_by_strain)

datatable(adonis_by_strain, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Strain", "R²", "F statistic", "Permutated p value")) %>%
  formatPercentage(columns = "R2", digits = 2) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "F", digits = 3) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "p", digits = 3)

title: GIL01 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
In this section, we load the data necessary to begin the RNAseq analysis: counts and metadata. We also load genome annotation files to visualize the results.


Code
counts <- readRDS(file = "lytic_cycle_induction_counts.rds")
metadata <- readRDS(file = "lytic_cycle_induction_metadata.rds")
annotation <- readRDS(file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

title: GIL01 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC1
In the case of PC1, we can observe a clear separation into three parts, similar to that observed in PCA: samples from time 0, 10 and 60-mock, 30-minute samples and 60-minute samples. According to these results, it appears that induction at 10 minutes does not produce significant changes in gene expression, and the non-induced samples (60-mock samples) are very similar to those from early induction. Regarding the 20 most variable genes, as expected, there are large changes in the expression of the GIL01 genes, but also of the prophage, with the latter’s expression increasing particularly at 60 minutes.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC1), ], labels_row = labels_PC1, legend = FALSE)

title: GIL01 lytic cycle induction analysis
text:
PC2
In the case of PC2, we find a clear difference compared to PC1: 60-mock samples are associated with those from minute 60 and not with those from early induction. This could be interpreted as these genes explaining changes in expression associated with the passage of time, and not so much with the presence of GIL01. This is especially evident in the genes GBJ002_03168 to GBJ002_03171, which encode for arginine deiminase (arcA), ornithine carbamoyltransferase (argF), arginine-ornithine antiporter (arcD) and carbamate kinase (arcC), respectively. The opposite pattern appears to be observed for the genes GBJ002_03392 and GBJ002_03393, which encode for PTS trehalose transporter subunit IIBC (treB) and alpha,alpha-phosphotrehalase (treC), respectively.


Code
pheatmap(vs_mat[rownames(top_genes_PC2), ], labels_row = labels_PC2, legend = FALSE)

title: Strains GIL01 vs GBJ002 contrast
text:
In order to perform the GIL01 vs GBJ002 strains contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = c("Strain", "GIL01", "GBJ002"), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, only 35 upregulated and 38 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4750 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 35, 0.74%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 38, 0.8%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 4)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we only found positive enrichment related to gene expression, translation and certain metabolic processes.
Regarding MF, we only observed positive enrichment in a high number of functions, mostly related to the binding of RNA-associated molecules. Related functions were also favoured, such as catalytic activity on nucleic acids and ligase activity.
Regarding CC, only positive enrichments were found, mainly related to the cytoplasm, ribosomes and protein complexes.

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
Boxplots


Code
cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts))



GIL01
In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes.


Code
gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("GIL01_"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil01_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil01_annotation %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),
    Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description),
    Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype),
    Gene_ID = factor(
      Gene_ID,
      levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[
        gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID
      ]
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes(
  x = Gene_ID,
  y = CPM_log2,
  text = paste(
    "Gene ID:", Gene_ID,
    ifelse(
      Gene_symbol != "",
      paste0("<br>Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description != "",
      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",
      paste0("<br>Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype),
      ""
    ),
    "<br>Sample:", Sample,
    "<br>Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2)
  )
)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.25,
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.7,
    aes(color = Color)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  ylab("Gene expression") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil01_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 0 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, name = "Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, no DE genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 0, 0%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 0, 0%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 1)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.

contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GIL01 strains contrast at 10 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time10", "StrainGIL16.MMCTRUE"), c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time10", "StrainGIL01.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, no DE genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 0, 0%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 0, 0%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 678, 14%
(mean count < 9)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
Boxplots


Code
cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts))



GIL01
In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes.


Code
gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("GIL01_t10"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil01_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil01_annotation %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),
    Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description),
    Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype),
    Gene_ID = factor(
      Gene_ID,
      levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[
        gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID
      ]
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes(
  x = Gene_ID,
  y = CPM_log2,
  text = paste(
    "Gene ID:", Gene_ID,
    ifelse(
      Gene_symbol != "",
      paste0("<br>Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description != "",
      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",
      paste0("<br>Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype),
      ""
    ),
    "<br>Sample:", Sample,
    "<br>Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2)
  )
)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.25,
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.7,
    aes(color = Color)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  ylab("Gene expression") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil01_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








GIL16
In the case of GIL16, the results are similar. Again, the major capsid protein gene (GIL16_18) is the most highly expressed. Although there is greater variation between samples in this case, GIL16-DNT_01 exhibits expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL16_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL16_05) genes.

  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%

    cols = starts_with("GIL16_t10"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil16_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL16", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil16_long_cpm <- gil16_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil16_annotation %>%

  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),

    ),

      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",

)) +

Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 30 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time30", "StrainGIL16.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 85 upregulated and 150 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 291 genes were discarded due to low counts, likely because they are expressed in other contrasts. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we found positive enrichment in processes related to translation, gene expression and proteins and proteins, macromolecules and ketones metabolic processes. Regarding negative enrichments, they are based on ions transport through heme ABC transporter substrate-binding protein IsdE (GBJ002_01760) and heme uptake proteins IsdC (GBJ002_01761 and GBJ002_01763) genes.
Regarding MF, we only observed positive enrichments in functions related to the binding of RNA-associated molecules.
Regarding CC, only positive enrichments were found, mainly related to the cytosol, ribosomes and protein complexes.

title: Strains GIL01 vs GBJ002 contrast
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL01 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 60 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time60", "StrainGIL01.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 287 upregulated and 416 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.

Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GIL01 strains contrast at 60 minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time60", "StrainGIL16.MMCTRUE"), c("Strain_GIL01_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL01.Time60", "StrainGIL01.MMCTRUE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, only 2 upregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)

LFC < -0.58 (down) : 0, 0%

          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",

            scrollX = TRUE,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Strains GIL16 vs GIL01
text:
Boxplots


Code
cpm_df <- as.data.frame(cpm(full_counts))



GIL01
In GIL01, we found that the most highly expressed gene is GIL01_18, which corresponds to the major capsid protein gene. It is also worth highlighting a de novo transcript identified by StringTie on the negative strand (GIL01-DNT_01), which shows expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL01_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) genes.


Code
gil01_long_cpm <- cpm_df %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("Gene_ID") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("GIL01_t60"),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "CPM"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(CPM_log2 = log2(CPM + 1))

gil01_annotation <- annotation %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^GIL01", Gene_ID)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_number = as.numeric(sub(".*_(\\d+)$", "\\1", Gene_ID)),
    Color = dplyr::case_when(
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 1 & Gene_number <= 8  ~ "red",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
      Strand == "-" ~ "blue",
      TRUE ~ "black"
    )
  )

gil01_long_cpm <- gil01_long_cpm %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    gil01_annotation %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Color, Gene_symbol, Description, Subtype),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    Gene_symbol = ifelse(is.na(Gene_symbol), "", Gene_symbol),
    Description = ifelse(is.na(Description), "", Description),
    Subtype = ifelse(is.na(Subtype), "-", Subtype),
    Gene_ID = factor(
      Gene_ID,
      levels = gil01_annotation$Gene_ID[
        gil01_annotation$Gene_ID %in% Gene_ID
      ]
    )
  )

p <- ggplot(gil01_long_cpm, aes(
  x = Gene_ID,
  y = CPM_log2,
  text = paste(
    "Gene ID:", Gene_ID,
    ifelse(
      Gene_symbol != "",
      paste0("<br>Gene symbol: ", Gene_symbol),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description != "",
      paste0("<br>Description: ", Description),
      ""
    ),
    ifelse(
      Description == "Hypothetical protein",
      paste0("<br>Phynteny annotation: ", Subtype),
      ""
    ),
    "<br>Sample:", Sample,
    "<br>Gene expression:", round(CPM_log2, 2)
  )
)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(
    width = 0.25,
    size = 1.5,
    alpha = 0.7,
    aes(color = Color)
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  ylab("Gene expression") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ceiling(max(gil01_long_cpm$CPM_log2, na.rm = TRUE)))) +
  scale_color_identity() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")








GIL16
In the case of GIL16, the results are similar. Again, the major capsid protein gene (GIL16_18) is the most highly expressed. Although there is greater variation between samples in this case, GIL16-DNT_01 exhibits expression levels similar to genome terminal protein (GIL16_04) and DNA polymerase (GIL16_05) genes.

      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 9 & Gene_number <= 30 ~ "darkred",
      Strand == "+" & !is.na(Gene_number) & Gene_number >= 33 & Gene_number <= 36 ~ "grey",
