title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
To analyze differential expression across strains and time points, we performed all statistical modeling within a single DESeq2 dataset rather than creating separate DESeq2 objects for each time point. Because the experimental design includes strong temporal dynamics associated with phage induction, library composition changes substantially across conditions. Analyzing all samples together allows DESeq2 to estimate size factors and dispersion parameters using the full dataset, thereby borrowing statistical strength across conditions and ensuring a consistent normalization framework. Importantly, replicate effects (treated as a batch factor in the model) are captured globally; splitting the dataset by time would force DESeq2 to re‑estimate normalization and dispersion independently for each subset, disrupting the shared technical structure of the experiment and potentially introducing artificial differences. Using a unified DESeq2 object therefore provides a coherent, statistically robust foundation that preserves biological comparability, controls technical variation and enables accurate strain‑level contrasts at each time point.
In order to perform the GIL16 vs GBJ002 strains contrast at 60-mock minutes, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Strain_GIL16_vs_GBJ002", "StrainGIL16.Time60")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 153 upregulated and 637 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)

adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 153, 3.1%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 637, 13%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 1)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

KEGG

Negative enrichments were associated with nucleotide and aromatic amino acid metabolism, replication and repair and cell motility pathways.

title: Strains GIL16 vs GBJ002
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we find positive enrichments in processes related to translation, gene expression and diverse metabolic processes. Negative enrichments were associated with aromatic amino acids and amines metabolic processes.
Regarding MF, we only observed positive enrichments in functions related to the binding of nucleic acids molecules and structural functions.
Regarding CC, only positive enrichments were found, mainly related to the cytosol, ribosomes and protein complexes.



Code
go_bp_up <- go_enrichment(up_genes, all_genes, "BP")

title: Time points 10 vs 0 contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 10 vs 0 contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = list(c("Time_10_vs_0", "MMC_TRUE_vs_FALSE")), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 20 upregulated and 7 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 484 genes were discarded due to low counts, likely because they are expressed in latter times or in the presence of GIL01 or GIL16. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 20, 0.4%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 7, 0.14%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 484, 9.7%
(mean count < 12)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Time points 10 vs 0 contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we observed positive enrichments in processes related to DNA damage and stress response.
Regarding MF, we found positive enrichments in functions related to energy and nucleic acid metabolism.

title: Time points 60 vs 0 contrast
text:
KEGG
According to KEGG categories, we found positive and negative enrichments. In the case of the former, they were mainly related to metabolism, but also to replication and repair processes, specifically nucleotide excision repair, due to GBJ002_01735 (excinuclease ABC subunit C), GBJ002_02363 (excinuclease ABC subunit UvrA), GBJ002_02364 (excinuclease ABC subunit B), GBJ002_03098 (DNA helicase PcrA), GBJ002_03099 (NAD-dependent DNA ligase LigA) and GBJ002_03820 (RNA polymerase recycling motor HelD) genes. In the case of the latter, it resulted in depletion of more metabolic pathways than in previous contrast. Interestingly, an enrichment appears in Human diseases, specifically in the beta-lactam resistance pathway.

According to BP, positive enrichments were mainly related to nucleotide metabolism, while negative enrichment was found in the biosynthetic processes of amino acids, carboxylic acids, lipids and other small molecules.

title: Time points 60-mock vs 0 contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 60-mock vs 0 contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = c("Time", "60", "0"), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 488 upregulated and 394 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 4990 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 488, 9.8%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 394, 7.9%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 3)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Time points 60-mock vs 0 contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, positive enrichment was mainly related to amino acids, carbohydrates and nucleic acids metabolism, while negative enrichment was found in branched-chain and pyruvate family amino acids metabolism and fatty acids biosynthetic processes.
Regarding MF, we found a positive enrichment in oxidoreductase activity.
Regarding CC, we found a positive enrichment in oxidoreductase complex.

title: Time points 60-mock vs 0 contrast
text:
EC
The EC classification establishes up to four levels to define enzymes. In this section, the first three were analyzed, achieving statistical significance in all of them. In the first level, oxidoreductases (1) and isomerases (5) numbers were positively and negatively enriched by 32 and 15 genes, respectively. In the second, 1.4 level was positively enriched by 5 genes and 1.7 negatively enriched by 7 genes. Lastly, in the third, only negative enrichment in 1.7.99 level was observed by 4 out of 4 genes, corresponding to GBJ002_00573 (hydroxylamine reductase), GBJ002_04840 (nitrate reductase subunit alpha), GBJ002_04841 (nitrate reductase subunit beta) and GBJ002_04843 (respiratory nitrate reductase subunit gamma) genes.

title: Time points 30 vs 0 contrast
text:
KEGG
According to KEGG categories, we found positive and negative enrichments. In the case of the former, they were mainly related to metabolism, but also to replication and repair processes, specifically nucleotide excision repair, due to GBJ002_01735 (excinuclease ABC subunit C), GBJ002_02363 (excinuclease ABC subunit UvrA), GBJ002_02364 (excinuclease ABC subunit B), GBJ002_03098 (DNA helicase PcrA), GBJ002_03099 (NAD-dependent DNA ligase LigA) and GBJ002_03820 (RNA polymerase recycling motor HelD) genes. In the case of the latter, it resulted in depletion of metabolic pathways related to amino acid and carbohydrates, among other compounds, in addition to pathways related to cell motility, membrane transport by ABC transporters and beta-lactam resistance.

According to BP, we again observed positive enrichments in processes related to DNA damage and stress response. Negative enrichment was found in biological processes mainly associated with amino acids.
Regarding MF, we again found positive enrichment in processes related to energy and nucleic acid metabolism, while the transmembrane transporter activity of different compounds was depleted.

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 60 vs 60-mock contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, name = "MMC_TRUE_vs_FALSE", lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 751 upregulated and 600 downregulated genes were obtained. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.
Code
summary(contrast, alpha = 0.05)



out of 5023 with nonzero total read count
adjusted p-value < 0.05
LFC > 0.58 (up)    : 751, 15%
LFC < -0.58 (down) : 600, 12%
outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
low counts [2]     : 0, 0%
(mean count < 0)
[1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
[2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results


Code
datatable(contrast_df[!is.na(contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value) & contrast_df$Adjusted_p_value < 0.05, ],
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Gene symbol", "Description", "COG category / Homologue", "Association",
          "Type", "Subtype", "Source", "Mean normalized counts", "LFC", "Adjusted p value"), rownames = FALSE,
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2, rightColumns = 2),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("Mean_normalized_counts", "LFC"), digits = 2) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = "Adjusted_p_value", digits = 3)

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
GO
The Gene Ontology (GO) is organized into three categories: Biological Process (BP), which describes the biological objectives to which gene products contribute; Molecular Function (MF), which refers to the elemental activities of gene products at the molecular level; and Cellular Component (CC), which specifies the locations within the cell where gene products are active.

According to BP, we observed positive enrichment in processes related to cellular response to stimuli, DNA processes and ions transport, among others. Negative enrichment was found in metabolic processes associated with amino acids and other compounds, such as organic acids and small molecules.
Regarding MF, we only found positive enrichment in processes related to transporter, DNA-associated and RNA binding activities.
Regarding CC, positive enrichments were related to ribosomes, intracelullar organelles and protein-containing complexes, among other components.

title: Time points 60 vs 60-mock contrast
text:
EC
The EC classification establishes up to four levels to define enzymes. In this section, the first three were analyzed, achieving statistical significance in all of them for positive enrichment. In the first level, oxidoreductases (1) and translocases (7) numbers were enriched by 36 and 14 DE genes, respectively. In the second, 5.6 level was enriched by 9 genes. Lastly, in the third, 5.6.2 level was enriched by 9 out of 16 genes, corresponding to GBJ002_00759 (DNA topoisomerase IV subunit A), GBJ002_00760 (DNA topoisomerase IV subunit B), GBJ002_00997 (replicative DNA helicase), GBJ002_01078 (primosomal protein N’), GBJ002_02711 (replicative DNA helicase), GBJ002_02736 (DNA topoisomerase (ATP-hydrolyzing) subunit B), GBJ002_02737 (DNA gyrase subunit A), GBJ002_03710 (replicative DNA helicase) and GBJ002_03905 (helicase-exonuclease AddAB subunit AddA) genes.

title: Time points 60-mock vs 0 contrast
text:
In order to perform the time 60-mock vs 0 contrast, we established a log₂ fold-change (LFC) threshold of ±0.585. This value reflects an increase or decrease in expression of 50%, depending on whether it is positive or negative, respectively. The default value is 0, but when performing the other contrasts we obtained too many differentially expressed (DE) genes. This violates one of DESeq2’s key assumptions, which is that most genes are not differentially expressed.
Code
contrast <- results(dds, contrast = c("Time", "60", "0"), lfcThreshold = log2(1.5))
contrast_df <- as.data.frame(contrast)

contrast_df <- contrast_df %>%
  { x <- .; x$Gene_ID <- rownames(x); x } %>%
  left_join(
    annotation %>%
      filter(Gene_ID %in% rownames(contrast_df)) %>%
      dplyr::select(Gene_ID, Gene_symbol, Description, Classification, Association, Type, Subtype, Source),
    by = "Gene_ID"
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Mean_normalized_counts = baseMean,
    LFC = log2FoldChange,
    Adjusted_p_value = padj
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Gene_ID,
    Gene_symbol,
    Description,
    Classification,
    Association,
    Type,
    Subtype,
    Source,
    Mean_normalized_counts,
    LFC,
    Adjusted_p_value
  )
As can be seen in the summary() results, 274 upregulated and 358 downregulated genes were obtained. In addition, 195 genes were discarded due to low counts, probably because they are expressed in inducted samples or in the absence of GIL16. Below is an interactive table where all DE genes can be viewed individually.

title: GIL01 hierarchical clustering
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps. In this case, we used rho proportionality to measure coexpression among GBJ002 (chromosome and pBtic235) and GIL01 genes and GIL01 samples at time points 0, 10, 30 and 60, excluding 60-mock samples, since they are not induced by MMC and may introduce noise.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[1:4967, 13:24]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

title: GIL01 hierarchical clustering
text:
COG
Regarding COG categories, 9 clusters show significant enrichment comprising 7 distinct terms. Among the most notable findings are GIL01_C032b, which is enriched in genes related to cell motility (N), and GIL01_C029a, enriched in mobilome: prophages, transposons (X) category.


Code
cog_results <- cog_enrichment_by_cluster(cluster_gene_table, all_genes)

datatable(cog_results, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("COG", "IN", "OUT", "Genes", "Odds ratio", "p value", "Adjusted p-value", "Cluster"),

title: GIL01 hierarchical clustering
text:
KEGG
Regarding KEGG terms, there is a wide variety across the clusters. GIL01_C032b is again related to cell motility pathways, while GIL01_C029b, GIL01_C020c and GIL01_C020d are realted to membrane transport pathways. Regarding replication and repair pathways, we can observe two distinct clusters: GIL01_C290, associated to DNA replication, homologous recombination and mismatch repair, and GIL01_C036c, associated to nucleotide excision repair. Interestingly, 9 clusters appears to be enriched in diverse metabolic pathways, which suggests a specialization in gene expression in the different reflected pathways.
