title: Hierarchical clusterings comparisons
text:
In this section we load the clusters data obtained for each of the three hierarchical clusterings obtained in the previous pages.


Code
gbj002_clusters <- readRDS(file = "gbj002_clusters.rds")
gil01_clusters <- readRDS(file = "gil01_clusters.rds")
gil16_clusters <- readRDS(file = "gil16_clusters.rds")

gbj002_clusters_stability <- readRDS(file = "gbj002_clusters_stability.rds")
gil01_clusters_stability <- readRDS(file = "gil01_clusters_stability.rds")
gil16_clusters_stability <- readRDS(file = "gil16_clusters_stability.rds")

title: Hierarchical clusterings comparisons
text:
GIL01 vs GBJ002 heatmaps

Gene ratio on GIL01 clusters
This heatmap reflects the gene ratio on GIL01 clusters within GBJ002 clusters. Between the two largest clusters in GBJ002 (GBJ002_C384 and GBJ002_C281), we can observe a set of bright colors in the columns, reflecting that the genes in these clusters have specialized in other clusters in GIL01. Additionally, GBJ002_C072 has subdivided into two clusters in GIL01: GIL01_C040 and GIL01_C020e. This subdivision is primarily due to gene location, as the genes in the first cluster are located upstream of those in the second cluster. In addition, a high gene ratio (0.710) between GBJ002_C235 and GIL01_C290 is observed, which is mainly due to the presence of pBtic235 genes.


Code
gil01_gbj002_heatmaps$heatmap_rows








Gene ratio on GBJ002 clusters
This heatmap reflects the gene ratio on GBJ002 clusters within GIL01 clusters. On this occasion, we only found that the largest GIL01 cluster (GIL01_C290) is more specialized in GBJ002, specifically in GBJ002_C235 and GBJ002_C029a. Additionally, other gene ratios greater than 50% could be highlighted, as occurs between GIL01_C054 and GBJ002_C029c (gene ratio = 0.793) and GIL01_C055 and GBJ002_C034 (gene ratio = 0.588).


Code
gil01_gbj002_heatmaps$heatmap_columns

title: Hierarchical clusterings comparisons
text:
GIL16 vs GBJ002 heatmaps

Gene ratio on GIL16 clusters
This heatmap reflects the gene ratio on GIL16 clusters within GBJ002 clusters. Between the two largest clusters in GBJ002 (GBJ002_C384 and GBJ002_C281), we can observe a set of bright colors in the columns, reflecting that the genes in these clusters have specialized in other clusters in GIL16. GBJ002_C072 was subdivided into GIL01_C040 and GIL01_C020e, but on this occassion only a high conservation (gene ratio = 0.757) is found with GIL16_C074. In view of the genes shared between these clusters, the results suggest that coexpression of these tRNA genes is similar in GBJ002 and GIL16 strains. In addition, a high gene ratio (0.692) between GBJ002_C235 and GIL16_C289 is observed, which is mainly due to the presence of pBtic235 genes.


Code
gil16_gbj002_heatmaps$heatmap_rows








Gene ratio on GBJ002 clusters
This heatmap reflects the gene ratio on GBJ002 clusters within GIL16 clusters. On this occasion, we only found that the largest GIL16 cluster (GIL16_C289) is more specialized in GBJ002, specifically in GBJ002_C235 and GBJ002_C029a. Additionally, other gene ratios greater than 50% could be highlighted, as occurs between GIL16_C074 and GBJ002_C072 (gene ratio = 0.778) and GIL16_C062b and GBJ002_C029c (gene ratio = 0.828).


Code
gil16_gbj002_heatmaps$heatmap_columns

title: Hierarchical clusterings comparisons
text:
GIL16 vs GIL01 heatmaps

Gene ratio on GIL16 clusters
This heatmap reflects the gene ratio on GIL16 clusters within GIL01 clusters. This time, we don’t find specializations as high as in previous heatmaps. Instead, we observe some low to moderate conservations when we divide the graph along the main diagonal. The only notable conservation is between GIL16_C289 and GIL01_C290 (gene ratio = 0.824), whose shared genes are mostly from pBtic235. Also, a gene ratio = 0.421 can be observed between GIL16_C019 (cluster with the GIL16 genes expressed from the P1-P2 tandem promoters) and GIL01_C055 (cluster with the majority of GIL01 genes), which corresponds to GBJ002_01361 (magnesium-translocating P-type ATPase), GBJ002_01362 (Mg2+ transport ATPase C), GBJ002_03168 (arginine deiminase), GBJ002_03169 (ornithine carbamoyltransferase), GBJ002_03170 (arginine-ornithine antiporter), GBJ002_03171 (carbamate kinase), GBJ002_03173 (ABC transporter permease) and GBJ002_03410 (diacylglycerol kinase) genes. These genes, therefore, appear to be associated with the expression of tectiviruses.


Code
gil16_gil01_heatmaps$heatmap_rows








Gene ratio on GIL01 clusters
This heatmap reflects the gene ratio on GIL01 clusters within GIL16 clusters. Again, we observe some low to moderate conservations when we divide the graph along the main diagonal. However, on this occasion, some specializations appear to be observed in GIL01. For example, GIL16_C289 has subdivided into GIL01_C290 and GIL01_C029b and GIL16_C074 into GIL01_C040 and GIL01_C020e. Additionally, between GIL16_C062b and GIL01_C054 there is a gene ratio = 0.722, with most of the shared genes corresponding to ribosomal proteins.


Code
gil16_gil01_heatmaps$heatmap_columns

title: GIL01 network
text:
Correlation shows how two variables vary together. In the case of RNAseq data, these correlations are inevitably relative, since we don’t have the total number of reads, but rather a proportion of the total. Although algorithms like WGCNA employ Pearson correlation, this can lead to spurious associations, and more recent advances, especially in metagenomics, suggest the use of compositionally-aware methods. One such method is the rho (ρ) statistic, which can be used instead of correlation as the basis for familiar analyses and visualization methods, including coexpression networks and clustered heatmaps.
In the Hierarchical clustering section, we used all ρ values from 0, 10, 30 and 60 minute samples ​​to determine coexpression clusters. In this case, the goal is to create a network, and if we retain all values, the network will be fully connected and therefore uninformative. The propr package contains a function to determine the significance of proportionality using a built-in permutation procedure and estimate the false discovery rate (FDR). Although the code is not shown below due to its computationally intensive execution, to find a threshold where the FDR < 0.05, both positive and negative values ​​were considered and tests were performed to the second decimal place with up to 1000 permutation tests.
For the GIL01 networks, the selected threshold was 0.81, i.e., all coexpressions between -0.81 and 0.81 were considered null because they could be random. Subsequently, the rho values ​​were rounded to five decimal places, and the positive and negative coexpression matrices, along with their respective strength-associated matrices, were constructed. Since edge weights in networks are usually interpreted as distances between nodes, the positive matrix was reconstructed so that the weights were 1 - ρ. However, for the strength matrix, since this parameter is associated with the edge weight, the same conversion was performed again to calculate this parameter. For the negative coexpression matrix, since the values ​​are obviously negative, the conversion was performed as 1 + ρ, so that very strong negative coexpressions would tend towards 0 (although they would never be 0 in any case). For the strength-associated matrix, the conversion was the same as that performed for the strength-associated positive matrix.


Code
pseudocount <- min(counts[counts > 0])/10
counts <- counts + pseudocount
counts <- counts[1:4967, 13:24]
all_genes <- rownames(counts)

rhos <- propr(t(counts), metric = "rho")

rhos_mat <- rhos@matrix
threshold <- 0.81
rhos_mat[ rhos_mat > -threshold & rhos_mat < threshold ] <- 0
rhos_mat <- round(rhos_mat, 5)

pos_mat <- rhos_mat
pos_mat[pos_mat < threshold] <- 0
pos_mat <- ifelse(pos_mat != 0, 1 - pos_mat, pos_mat)
pos_mat_strength <- ifelse(pos_mat != 0, 1 - pos_mat, pos_mat)

neg_mat <- rhos_mat
neg_mat[neg_mat > -threshold] <- 0
neg_mat <- ifelse(neg_mat != 0, neg_mat + 1, neg_mat)
neg_mat_strength <- ifelse(neg_mat != 0, 1 - neg_mat, neg_mat)

title: GIL01 network
text:
Positive network

Full-network attributes
This section will display the main full-network attributes. While not very informative on their own, they will allow us to compare these metrics across different networks. An additional table is also included, showing the number of components in which the network is divided and the size (number of genes) of each component. In this case, we see that 89.97% of the genes (4469 out of 4967) are interconnected somehow. Conversely, 7.57% of the genes (376 out of 4469) are not coexpressed with any other genes. The remaining genes are distributed in components of intermediate sizes, i.e., they are not isolated, but are coexpressed with few genes.


Code
order_pos <- gorder(g_pos)
size_pos <- gsize(g_pos)
mean_degree_pos <- 2 * (size_pos/order_pos)
assortativity_pos <- assortativity_degree(g_pos)
density_pos <- edge_density(g_pos)
diameter_pos <- diameter(g_pos)
avg_path_length_pos <- mean_distance(g_pos)
ta_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "average")
tg_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "global")
ge_pos <- global_efficiency(g_pos)

df_pos <- data.frame(
  Order = order_pos,
  Size = size_pos,
  Mean_degree = round(mean_degree_pos, 2),
  Assortativity = round(assortativity_pos, 3),
  Density = round(density_pos, 3),
  Diameter = round(diameter_pos, 2),
  Average_path_length = round(avg_path_length_pos, 3),
  Average_clustering_coefficient = round(ta_pos, 3),
  Global_transitivity = round(tg_pos, 3),
  Global_efficiency = round(ge_pos, 2),
  row.names = "Positive network"
)

datatable(t(df_pos), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
components_pos_df <- as.data.frame(table(Size = table(components(g_pos)$membership)))
colnames(components_pos_df)[2] <- "Components"
components_pos_df <- components_pos_df[order(components_pos_df$Components), c("Components", "Size")]
datatable(components_pos_df, rownames = FALSE)








Per-node attributes


Code
degree_pos <- degree(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
strength_pos <- strength(g_pos_strength)
betweenness_pos <- betweenness(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
coreness_pos <- coreness(g_pos)
tl_pos <- transitivity(g_pos, type = "local")
triangles_pos <- setNames(count_triangles(g_pos), V(g_pos)$name)
harmonic_centrality_pos <- harmonic_centrality(g_pos, normalized = TRUE)
eig_pos <- eigen_centrality(g_pos)$vector
pr_pos <- page_rank(g_pos)$vector
apl_pos <- apl(g_pos)



Degree
The degree of a vertex is its most basic structural property, the number of its adjacent edges. In the lower histogram, which can also be called the degree distribution, we see how many genes have a given normalized degree (degree divided by the network size minus 1) in each interval. As is often the case in biological networks, most nodes have few connections and only a few have many connections, i.e., a small fraction of genes are coexpressed with many other genes.


Code
network_hist(degree_pos, xlab = "Degree")











Strength
The strength of a node is calculated by summing the edge weights of the adjacent edges for each node. While conceptually similar to degree, at the gene level it allows us to discern whether coexpressions with other genes are strong or weak, whereas in the case of degree, the attribute is more boolean, indicating whether or not a gene coexpression exists. Comparing strength distribution with degree distribution reveals a similar pattern, with few genes exhibiting high strength.


Code
network_hist(strength_pos, xlab = "Strength")











Betweenness
Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between other pairs of nodes in the network. In other words, it captures the extent to which a node acts as an intermediary in connecting different parts of the network. In most networks, the normalized betweenness distribution is highly uneven: the majority of nodes have very low betweenness, while only a few nodes concentrate high values. Nodes with high betweenness can be interpreted as bottlenecks, since many shortest paths rely on them; as a result, they may play a crucial role in controlling information flow, i.e., gene expression.


Code
network_hist(betweenness_pos, xlab = "Betweenness")











Coreness
The k-core of graph is a maximal subgraph in which each vertex has at least degree k. The coreness of a vertex is k if it belongs to the k-core but not to the (k+1)-core. In this case, we see three distinct groups: genes with low coreness, genes in intermediate k-cores and genes belonging to high k-cores. The first group will be genes that have few positive coexpressions with other genes, while the last group will be those closely related to each other.


Code
network_hist(coreness_pos, xlab = "Coreness")











Clustering coefficient (C)
The clustering coefficient (C) is a local metric that measures the level of clustering of the nodes. In this case, the distribution approximates a normal distribution, although with longer tails. In any case, it is observed that there are few genes with a C > 0.9. However, a high value does not necessarily reflect that a node is important, since it may be isolated nodes that, conceptually, form a perfect cluster, since they are grouped with each other. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(tl_pos, xlab = "Clustering coefficient (C)")











Triangles
Triangles reflect the number of triangles a node is part of, i.e., the number of sets of three nodes that a particular node belongs to. In this case, a distribution with high positive skewness is observed. In other words, few nodes form many triangles with other nodes.


Code
network_hist(triangles_pos, xlab = "Triangles")











Harmonic centrality
The harmonic centrality of a vertex is the mean inverse distance to all other vertices. The inverse distance to an unreachable vertex is considered to be zero. Since some nodes are disconnected, this metric allows us to determine how central their position is in the network. In this way, it is the equivalent of closeness for disconnected networks. In the histogram below, we can observe that most nodes have a harmonic centrality between 0 and 5, while only six exceed this latter value. In other words, only a few genes occupy a very central position (i.e., are close to the rest) in the positive coexpression network.


Code
network_hist(harmonic_centrality_pos, xlab = "Harmonic centrality")











Eigenvector centrality
Eigenvector centrality measures how important a node is in a network by considering not only how many connections it has, but also how important its neighbors are. A node gets a high score if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. This reflects a recursive idea: being connected to important nodes makes a node more important in return. As a result, nodes with high eigenvector centrality tend to belong to dense groups or cliques, where many nodes are interconnected. From a mathematical perspective, these scores come from the main eigenvector of the network’s adjacency matrix, which captures the strongest overall pattern of connections in the network.
In the lower histogram, we observe that more than 90% of the nodes have a low eigenvector centrality, while only 1.05% exceed a value of 0.8.


Code
network_hist(eig_pos, xlab = "Eigenvector centrality", n_intervals = 5)











PageRank
PageRank measures how central or important a node is based on the idea that connections from important nodes contribute more than connections from less important ones, even when the network has no direction. In an undirected network, PageRank works similarly to eigenvector centrality: a node has a high PageRank if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. The score can be interpreted as the probability that a random walker moving along edges (and occasionally jumping to a random node) will be found at that node. Therefore, nodes with high PageRank tend to lie in dense or well-connected parts of the network, rather than being isolated or peripheral.
In this case, the distribution is much more platykurtic than for eigenvector centrality, although there are still few genes with high values.


Code
network_hist(pr_pos, xlab = "PageRank", n_intervals = 5)











Average path length (L)
Average path length (also known as average shortest path length or characteristic path) for a node describes how far is, on average, from all other nodes in the network. It is computed as the mean of the shortest path distances from that node to every other reachable node. In simple terms, it tells us how many steps are typically needed to reach the rest of the network starting from this node. A small average path length indicates that the node is centrally located and can reach others efficiently, while a large value suggests that the node lies in a more peripheral position, requiring more intermediaries to connect to the rest of the network.
In this case, the distribution is heavily centered on values between 0.4 and 1. The most important nodes regarding this attribute are those with the lowest L values. Therefore, this attribute is expected to be negatively correlated with the other centrality values ​​obtained. In any case, this attribute is not particularly informative, since the nodes with lowest L values could be in low order components. Hence, this attribute should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(apl_pos, xlab = "Average path length (L)")












Per-edge attributes
Edge betweenness centrality measures how often an edge lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between pairs of nodes in the network. Conceptually, it quantifies the extent to which an edge acts as a bridge for connecting different parts of the network. Edges with high betweenness can be interpreted as critical links or bottlenecks, since their removal may strongly affect network connectivity or information flow. From a biological point of view, the edges could be interactions between proteins that, when eliminated, would alter the coexpression network.
The table below shows the edge betweenness values ​​for each pair of genes that exhibit positive coexpression. The highest values within each of the six gene-pair categories are obtained for the following combinations:

Chromosome–chromosome: DNA alkylation repair protein (GBJ002_02142) gene with cell wall metabolism sensor histidine kinase WalK (GBJ002_02704) gene.
Chromosome–pBtic235: winged helix DNA-binding domain protein (GBJ002_00399) gene with hypothetical protein (GBJ002_05065) gene.
Chromosome-GIL01: bacterial transcription activator effector binding domain-containing protein (GBJ002_02156) gene with DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) gene.
pBtic235–pBtic235: two hypothetical protein (GBJ002_05056 and GBJ002_05060) genes.
pBtic235-GIL01: hypothetical protein (GBJ002_05231) gene with head and packaging (GIL01_34) gene.
GIL01-GIL01: SSB (GIL01_02) gene with DNA polymerase (GIL01_05) gene.



Code
edge_betweenness_pos <- igraph::as_data_frame(g_pos, what = "edges")
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id <- seq_len(size_pos)
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_betweenness <- edge_betweenness(g_pos)
edge_betweenness_pos <- edge_betweenness_pos[order(edge_betweenness_pos$edge_betweenness, decreasing = TRUE), ]
rownames(edge_betweenness_pos) <- as.character(edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id)
edge_betweenness_pos$edge_id <- NULL

datatable(edge_betweenness_pos, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Gene 1", "Gene 2", "Weight (1 - ρ)", "Edge betweenness"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "weight", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "edge_betweenness", digits = 2)

title: GIL01 network
text:
Negative network

Full-network attributes
This section will display the main full-network attributes. While not very informative on their own, they will allow us to compare these metrics across different networks. An additional table is also included, showing the number of components in which the network is divided and the size (number of genes) of each component. In this case, we see that 71.33% of the genes (3543 out of 4967) are interconnected somehow. Conversely, 26.27% of the genes (1305 out of 4967) are not negatively coexpressed with any other genes. The remaining genes are distributed in components of intermediate sizes, i.e., they are not isolated, but are coexpressed with few genes.


Code
order_neg <- gorder(g_neg)
size_neg <- gsize(g_neg)
mean_degree_neg <- 2 * (size_neg/order_neg)
assortativity_neg <- assortativity_degree(g_neg)
density_neg <- edge_density(g_neg)
diameter_neg <- diameter(g_neg)
avg_path_length_neg <- mean_distance(g_neg)
ta_neg <- transitivity(g_neg, type = "average")
tg_neg <- transitivity(g_neg, type = "global")
ge_neg <- global_efficiency(g_neg)

df_neg <- data.frame(
  Order = order_neg,
  Size = size_neg,
  Mean_degree = round(mean_degree_neg, 2),
  Assortativity = round(assortativity_neg, 3),
  Density = round(density_neg, 3),
  Diameter = round(diameter_neg, 2),
  Average_path_length = round(avg_path_length_neg, 3),
  Average_clustering_coefficient = ta_neg,
  Global_transitivity = tg_neg,
  Global_efficiency = round(ge_neg, 2),
  row.names = "Negative network"
)

datatable(t(df_neg), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
components_neg_df <- as.data.frame(table(Size = table(components(g_neg)$membership)))
colnames(components_neg_df)[2] <- "Components"
components_neg_df <- components_neg_df[order(components_neg_df$Components), c("Components", "Size")]
datatable(components_neg_df, rownames = FALSE)








Per-node attributes


Code
degree_neg <- degree(g_neg, normalized = TRUE)
strength_neg <- strength(g_neg_strength)
betweenness_neg <- betweenness(g_neg, normalized = TRUE)
coreness_neg <- coreness(g_neg)
tl_neg <- transitivity(g_neg, type = "local")
triangles_neg <- setNames(count_triangles(g_neg), V(g_neg)$name)
harmonic_centrality_neg <- harmonic_centrality(g_neg, normalized = TRUE)
eig_neg <- eigen_centrality(g_neg)$vector
pr_neg <- page_rank(g_neg)$vector
apl_neg <- apl(g_neg)



Degree
The degree of a vertex is its most basic structural property, the number of its adjacent edges. In the lower histogram, which can also be called the degree distribution, we see how many genes have a given normalized degree (degree divided by the network size minus 1) in each interval. As is often the case in biological networks, most nodes have few connections and only a few have many connections, i.e., a small fraction of genes are negatively coexpressed with many other genes.


Code
network_hist(degree_neg, xlab = "Degree")











Strength
The strength of a node is calculated by summing the edge weights of the adjacent edges for each node. While conceptually similar to degree, at the gene level it allows us to discern whether coexpressions with other genes are strong or weak, whereas in the case of degree, the attribute is more boolean, indicating whether or not a gene coexpression exists. Comparing strength distribution with degree distribution reveals a similar pattern, with few genes exhibiting high strength.


Code
network_hist(strength_neg, xlab = "Strength")











Betweenness
Betweenness measures how often a node lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between other pairs of nodes in the network. In other words, it captures the extent to which a node acts as an intermediary in connecting different parts of the network. In most networks, the normalized betweenness distribution is highly uneven: the majority of nodes have very low betweenness, while only a few nodes concentrate high values. Nodes with high betweenness can be interpreted as bottlenecks, since many shortest paths rely on them; as a result, they may play a crucial role in controlling information flow, i.e., gene expression.


Code
network_hist(betweenness_neg, xlab = "Betweenness")











Coreness
The k-core of graph is a maximal subgraph in which each vertex has at least degree k. The coreness of a vertex is k if it belongs to the k-core but not to the (k+1)-core. In this case, we see three distinct groups: genes with low coreness, genes in intermediate k-cores and genes belonging to high k-cores. The first group will be genes that have few negative coexpressions with other genes, while the last group will be those closely related to each other.


Code
network_hist(coreness_neg, xlab = "Coreness")











Clustering coefficient (C)
The clustering coefficient (C) is a local metric that measures the level of clustering of the nodes. In this case, the distribution is uniform, with all nodes that have two or more neighbours showing C = 0. This is logical by definition, although it is better understood with the concept of triangles, explained below.


Code
sprintf("All %d nodes that have two or more neighbours show C = %s.", as.integer(table(tl_neg)), names(table(tl_neg)))


[1] "All 3176 nodes that have two or more neighbours show C = 0."




Triangles
Triangles reflect the number of triangles a node is part of, i.e., the number of sets of three nodes that a particular node belongs to. In this case, the distribution is uniform, since no node is included in any triangle. This is logical by definition, because if the interactions are negative, there cannot be triangles. Assuming three nodes A, B, and C, if A is negatively coexpressed with B and B is negatively coexpressed with C, then B could never be negatively coexpressed with C. Therefore, we will never obtain triangles in negative coexpression networks.


Code
sprintf("All %d nodes have %s triangles.", as.integer(table(triangles_neg)), names(table(triangles_neg)))


[1] "All 4967 nodes have 0 triangles."




Harmonic centrality
The harmonic centrality of a vertex is the mean inverse distance to all other vertices. The inverse distance to an unreachable vertex is considered to be zero. Since some nodes are disconnected, this metric allows us to determine how central their position is in the network. In this way, it is the equivalent of closeness for disconnected networks. In the histogram below, we can observe that most nodes have a harmonic centrality between 1 and 2 (although there is also a significant portion of nodes concentrated in values ​​close to 0, i.e., nodes not in the major component), while only four nodes exceed a value of 3. In other words, only a few genes occupy a very central position (i.e., are close to the rest) in the negative coexpression network.


Code
network_hist(harmonic_centrality_neg, xlab = "Harmonic centrality")











Eigenvector centrality
Eigenvector centrality measures how important a node is in a network by considering not only how many connections it has, but also how important its neighbors are. A node gets a high score if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. This reflects a recursive idea: being connected to important nodes makes a node more important in return. As a result, nodes with high eigenvector centrality tend to belong to dense groups or cliques, where many nodes are interconnected. From a mathematical perspective, these scores come from the main eigenvector of the network’s adjacency matrix, which captures the strongest overall pattern of connections in the network.
In the lower histogram, we observe that almost 82% of the nodes have a low eigenvector centrality, while nine genes exceed a value of 0.9.


Code
network_hist(eig_neg, xlab = "Eigenvector centrality")











PageRank
PageRank measures how central or important a node is based on the idea that connections from important nodes contribute more than connections from less important ones, even when the network has no direction. In an undirected network, PageRank works similarly to eigenvector centrality: a node has a high PageRank if it is connected to other nodes that are themselves well connected. The score can be interpreted as the probability that a random walker moving along edges (and occasionally jumping to a random node) will be found at that node. Therefore, nodes with high PageRank tend to lie in dense or well-connected parts of the network, rather than being isolated or peripheral.
In this case, the distribution is similar to the eigenvector centrality one and there are few genes with high values.


Code
network_hist(pr_neg, xlab = "PageRank")











Average path length (L)
Average path length (also known as average shortest path length or characteristic path) for a node describes how far is, on average, from all other nodes in the network. It is computed as the mean of the shortest path distances from that node to every other reachable node. In simple terms, it tells us how many steps are typically needed to reach the rest of the network starting from this node. A small average path length indicates that the node is centrally located and can reach others efficiently, while a large value suggests that the node lies in a more peripheral position, requiring more intermediaries to connect to the rest of the network.
In this case, the distribution is heavily centered on values between 0.4 and 0.6. The most important nodes regarding this attribute are those with the lowest L values. Therefore, this attribute is expected to be negatively correlated with the other centrality values ​​obtained. In any case, this attribute is not particularly informative, since the nodes with lowest L values could be in low order components. Hence, this attribute should be interpreted with caution.


Code
network_hist(apl_neg, xlab = "Average path length (L)")












Per-edge attributes
Edge betweenness centrality measures how often an edge lies on the shortest paths (geodesics) between pairs of nodes in the network. Conceptually, it quantifies the extent to which an edge acts as a bridge for connecting different parts of the network. Edges with high betweenness can be interpreted as critical links or bottlenecks, since their removal may strongly affect network connectivity or information flow. From a biological point of view, the edges could be interactions between proteins that, when eliminated, would alter the coexpression network.
The table below shows the edge betweenness values ​​for each pair of genes that exhibit negative coexpression. The highest values within each of the six gene-pair categories are obtained for the following combinations:

Chromosome–chromosome: formate–tetrahydrofolate ligase (GBJ002_04823) gene with peptidoglycan glycosyltransferase (GBJ002_05001) gene.
Chromosome–pBtic235: tetraprenyl‑beta‑curcumene synthase (GBJ002_01963) gene with phosphoesterase (GBJ002_05265) gene.
Chromosome-GIL01: T-box leader (BHPEJKKPJL_173) region with DNA, RNA and nucleotide metabolism (GIL01_08) gene.
pBtic235–pBtic235: Phr family secreted Rap phosphatase inhibitor (GBJ002_05148) gene with tRNA‑Leu(tag) (GBJ002_05161) gene.

No negative coexpressions were found between pBtic235-GIL01 and GIL01-GIL01 gene-pair categories.


Code
edge_betweenness_neg <- igraph::as_data_frame(g_neg, what = "edges")
edge_betweenness_neg$edge_id <- seq_len(size_neg)
edge_betweenness_neg$edge_betweenness <- edge_betweenness(g_neg)
edge_betweenness_neg <- edge_betweenness_neg[order(edge_betweenness_neg$edge_betweenness, decreasing = TRUE), ]
rownames(edge_betweenness_neg) <- as.character(edge_betweenness_neg$edge_id)
edge_betweenness_neg$edge_id <- NULL

datatable(edge_betweenness_neg, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c("Gene 1", "Gene 2", "Weight (1 - ρ)", "Edge betweenness"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "weight", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "edge_betweenness", digits = 2)

title: GIL01 network
text:
Networks comparison
This section compares the full-network attributes obtained for positive and negative coexpression networks. Since the same nodes (genes) are used in both cases, the order (number of genes) is expected to be the same. However, the size of the positive network is larger than that of the negative network, indicating more positive than negative coexpressions. Similarly, the mean degree of the positive network is higher.
Regarding the number of components, in both networks most nodes belong to a single component. However, the negative network has a higher proportion of isolated nodes.


Code
datatable(t(rbind(df_pos, df_neg)), rownames = c(
      "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
      "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
      "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
      colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))






Code
datatable(components_pos_df, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Positive network components")






Code
datatable(components_neg_df, rownames = FALSE, caption = "Negative network components")






The tables below show the values ​​of each per-node attribute for each of the nodes in the positive and negative networks.


Code
data_pos <- build_nodes_data(data = list(
      Degree = degree_pos,
      Strength = strength_pos,
      Betweenness = betweenness_pos,
      Coreness = coreness_pos,
      Clustering_coefficient = tl_pos,
      Triangles = triangles_pos,
      Harmonic_centrality = harmonic_centrality_pos,
      Eigenvector_centrality = eig_pos,
      PageRank = pr_pos,
      Average_path_length = apl_pos
))

data_neg <- build_nodes_data(data = list(
      Degree = degree_neg,
      Strength = strength_neg,
      Betweenness = betweenness_neg,
      Coreness = coreness_neg,
      Harmonic_centrality = harmonic_centrality_neg,
      Eigenvector_centrality = eig_neg,
      PageRank = pr_neg,
      Average_path_length = apl_neg
))

datatable(data_pos, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Gene", "Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Clustering coefficient (C)", "Triangles",
               "Harmonic centrality", "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank", "Average path length (L)"),
  caption = "Positive network per-node attributes",
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Degree", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Strength", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Betweenness", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Clustering_coefficient", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Harmonic_centrality", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Eigenvector_centrality", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "PageRank", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Average_path_length", digits = 3)






Code
datatable(data_neg, rownames = FALSE,
  colnames = c("Gene", "Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Harmonic centrality",
               "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank", "Average path length (L)"),
  caption = "Negative network per-node attributes",
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 1),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Degree", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Strength", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Betweenness", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Harmonic_centrality", digits = 3) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Eigenvector_centrality", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "PageRank", digits = 5) %>%
  formatRound(columns = "Average_path_length", digits = 3)






Below, two interactive heatmaps are shown, one for each network, displaying the Spearman correlations (ρ) between per-node attributes. Each correlation is computed using only those nodes for which both attributes are available, so the set of nodes contributing to each cell may vary across comparisons, reflecting the presence of missing values while maximizing statistical power. In the positive network, degree shows very high correlation with several measures, such as strength, coreness, harmonic centrality, PageRank and the number of triangles, indicating that these attributes largely capture the same topological dimension. In contrast, betweenness centrality exhibits only moderate association with degree, suggesting that it provides complementary information related to global network mediation. Eigenvector centrality shows an intermediate level of association with degree, indicating partial redundancy. A similar pattern is observed in the negative network, where degree is strongly correlated with most connectivity-based measures, including eigenvector centrality, reflecting the more centralized architecture of this network. In this case, betweenness centrality again shows substantially lower correlation with degree, highlighting its role as a distinct descriptor of node importance. Taken together, these results indicate that many commonly used attributes are highly redundant within each network.


Code
cor_mat_pos <- cor(data_pos[, 2:ncol(data_pos)], use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")
cor_mat_neg <- cor(data_neg[, 2:ncol(data_neg)], use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")

p_cor_mat_pos <- heatmaply(
      round(cor_mat_pos, 3),
      limits = c(-1, 1),
      dendrogram = "none",
      main = "Positive network nodes attributes associations",
      label_names = c("Row", "Column", "ρ"))

p_cor_mat_neg <- heatmaply(
      round(cor_mat_neg, 3),
      limits = c(-1, 1),
      dendrogram = "none",
      main = "Positive network nodes attributes associations",
      label_names = c("Row", "Column", "ρ"))

p_cor_mat_pos






Code
p_cor_mat_neg

title: Networks comparisons
text:
This section presents the full-network and per-node attributes of all previously generated networks. First, the full-network attributes will be compared, allowing for a non-statistical analysis of changes in the overall network attributes. Second, the distributions of the per-node attributes will be statistically compared using non-parametric tests.


Code
annotation <- readRDS(file = "GBJ002-GIL01-GIL16.rds")

load_networks_objects_rds()

rownames(df_pos_gbj002) <- "GBJ002"
rownames(df_neg_gbj002) <- "GBJ002"
rownames(df_pos_gil01) <- "GIL01"
rownames(df_neg_gil01) <- "GIL01"
rownames(df_pos_gil16) <- "GIL16"
rownames(df_neg_gil16) <- "GIL16"

title: Networks comparisons
text:
Positive networks
The table below shows the full-network attributes of the positive networks. The order differs among the three networks due to the incorporation of tectiviruses genes, although it is similar in all cases. In contrast, the size decreases by 67% and 76% in the GIL01 and GIL16 networks, respectively. This decreasing trend is also observed in parameters such as mean degree, density and global efficiency, reflecting a specialization in coexpression in increasing order: GBJ002 < GIL01 < GIL16. Other parameters, however, exhibit this reversed pattern. For example, assortativity is positive in all cases, reflecting a connection between similar nodes. The fact that it increases in the tectiviruses networks reflects a stronger tendency for nodes to connect with other nodes that share similar characteristics, i.e., greater specialization in gene coexpression. Other parameters, such as diameter or average path length, corroborate the above, as they reflect a greater distance between genes that are not closely related.


Code
datatable(t(rbind(df_pos_gbj002, df_pos_gil01, df_pos_gil16)), rownames = c(
    "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
    "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
    "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
  colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))

title: Networks comparisons
text:
Negative networks
The most drastic changes are observed in the negative networks. For example, the size decreases by 80% and 91% in the GIL01 and GIL16 networks, respectively. A similar trend can be seen in the mean degree, density and overall efficiency. In this case, assortativity decreases a 40% in the GIL01 network compared to GBJ002, but becomes negative (disassortative) with GIL16. Negative assortativity in a network means that nodes with low degrees tend to connect to nodes with high degrees, and vice versa, often resulting in a star-like or hub-and-spoke structure. This is especially important for finding gene expression regulators.


Code
datatable(t(rbind(df_neg_gbj002, df_neg_gil01, df_neg_gil16)), rownames = c(
    "Order", "Size", "Mean degree (<k>)", "Assortativity", "Density",
    "Diameter", "Average path length (L)", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
    "Global transitivity", "Global efficiency"),
  colnames = c("Full-network attributes" = 1))

title: Networks comparisons
text:
Positive networks

Distributions
In the distributions of the per-node attributes for the positive networks, we observe that all Friedman tests are significant except for PageRank. Kendall’s W indicates weak agreement for degree, strength, coreness, triangles, harmonic centrality and eigenvector centrality, and almost no agreement for betweenness, clustering coefficient and average path length. For betweenness, the Friedman test is significant, but the pairwise Wilcoxon test between GBJ002 and GIL01 isn’t. This may be because the deviations between these networks are extremely small. In other words, the Friedman p‑value becomes tiny not due to a meaningful effect, but because the sample size is very large (almost 5000 genes), making even negligible differences statistically detectable. Kendall’s W confirms this interpretation: the agreement between networks for betweenness is essentially zero (as we also see for the clustering coefficient or average path length), indicating that the effect is negligible despite the formal statistical significance. In practical terms, this suggests that network‑derived biological conclusions based on centrality measures with low concordance should be interpreted with caution, while those based on more stable metrics may reflect shared underlying biological organization across networks.


Code
datatable(distributions_pos,
          colnames = c(NULL, "Friedman p value", "Kendall's W", "GBJ002 vs GIL01", "GBJ002 vs GIL16", "GIL01 vs GIL16"),
          rownames = c("Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
                       "Triangles", "Harmonic centrality", "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank",
                       "Average path length (L)")) %>%
          formatSignif(columns = c("Friedman_p_value", "GBJ002_vs_GIL01",
                                   "GBJ002_vs_GIL16", "GIL01_vs_GIL16"), digits = 3) %>%
          formatRound(columns = "Kendalls_W", digits = 3) %>%
          formatStyle(columns = c("Friedman_p_value", "GBJ002_vs_GIL01",
                                  "GBJ002_vs_GIL16", "GIL01_vs_GIL16"),
                      backgroundColor = styleInterval(0.05, c("lightyellow", NA))) %>%
          formatStyle(columns = "Kendalls_W",
                      backgroundColor = styleInterval(c(0.1, 0.3, 0.5), c("white", "lightblue", "lightsalmon", "salmon")))








Differences by gene, attribute and networks pair
In the interactive table below, we can see, for each gene and its associated attributes in the positive networks, the difference between the attribute value in the first network compared to the second. Although it allows for a detailed view of the changes, it is recommended to view the relative changes in the table below, as this provides a clearer picture of the changes across all attributes. In contrast, this table is highly recommended for viewing the most variable genes as their attributes values increase or decrease from one network to another, so it is suggested to return to this table after viewing the rank_summary one, as it contains more details.
In this table, the normalized z-score difference corresponds to the difference for each gene and attribute between networks. The use of this metric is motivated by two main reasons. First, direct differences between raw attribute values do not account for the fact that a given value may be relatively high in one network but low in another. Second, transforming values into positional rankings would remove information about the magnitude of differences between genes within the same network.
For these reasons, the differences for each gene, attribute and pair of networks were computed as follows:

Z-score calculation within each network

For each gene \(i\) and attribute \(m\), the z-score is computed independently in each network:
\[
z_{1,i}^{(m)} = \frac{x_{1,i}^{(m)} - \mu_{1}^{(m)}}{\sigma_{1}^{(m)}}, \quad
z_{2,i}^{(m)} = \frac{x_{2,i}^{(m)} - \mu_{2}^{(m)}}{\sigma_{2}^{(m)}}
\]
where:

\(x_{k,i}^{(m)}\) is the value of attribute \(m\) for gene \(i\) in network \(k\).
\(\mu_{k}^{(m)}\) and \(\sigma_{k}^{(m)}\) are the mean and standard deviation of attribute \(m\) in network \(k\).


Normalization within each network

For each attribute \(m\), z-scores are normalized independently in each network by dividing by the maximum absolute z-score observed across all genes:
\[
\tilde{z}_{1,i}^{(m)} = \frac{z_{1,i}^{(m)}}{\max_j \left| z_{1,j}^{(m)} \right|}, \quad
\tilde{z}_{2,i}^{(m)} = \frac{z_{2,i}^{(m)}}{\max_j \left| z_{2,j}^{(m)} \right|}
\]
This rescales the values so that, for each network and attribute:
\[
\tilde{z}_{k,i}^{(m)} \in [-1, 1]
\]

Difference between normalized z-scores

The final normalized difference for gene \(i\), attribute \(m\) and a given pair of networks is then computed as:
\[
\Delta z_{i}^{(m)} = \tilde{z}_{2,i}^{(m)} - \tilde{z}_{1,i}^{(m)}
\]
With this formulation:

Genes with positive values (\(\Delta z > 0\)) have gained relative importance for a given attribute in network 2 compared to network 1.
Genes with negative values (\(\Delta z < 0\)) have decreased in relative importance.
The magnitude \(|\Delta z|\) reflects the strength of the change in relative position within each network.

This approach preserves both directionality and magnitude of change, while ensuring comparability across networks with potentially different distributions.


Code
datatable(na_diff_pos$diffs_long, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c(
  "Gene ID", "Pair", "Attribute", "Value on net 2", "Value on net 1", "Difference", "Normalized z-score difference"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("net_2", "net_1", "diff", "z_norm_diff"), digits = 3)








Differences by gene and networks pair according to attribute
In the interactive table below, we can see, for each gene, the difference between the values ​​of the respective attributes in the first positive network compared to those in the second. In some cases, missing values ​​appear because one or more values ​​associated with the attribute were missing from one or more of the networks.


Code
datatable(na_diff_pos$diffs_wide, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c(
  "Gene ID", "Pair", "Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Clustering coefficient (C)",
                       "Triangles", "Harmonic centrality", "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank",
                       "Average path length (L)"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("Degree_diff", "Strength_diff", "Betweenness_diff", "Clustering_coefficient_diff", "Harmonic_centrality_diff", "Eigenvector_centrality_diff", "PageRank_diff", "Average_path_length_diff"), digits = 3)








Magnitude of differences summary
Across the three pairwise positive networks comparisons, the results reveal clear and consistent structural differences. It is important to note that not all descriptive statistics are computed over the same genes, which means that comparisons between attributes must account for this variability.
Overall, the network pairs involving GBJ002 (GIL01–GBJ002 and GIL16–GBJ002) exhibit the largest absolute differences across most topological attributes. This trend is especially pronounced for measures linked to connectivity and node importance, such as strength, coreness and clustering coefficient. In these GBJ002-related comparisons, both the central tendency (mean and median absolute differences) and the measures of dispersion (SD, MAD, 95th percentile and maximum) are consistently higher. These results indicate substantial structural rewiring in the GBJ002 network relative to GIL01 and GIL16, affecting a wide range of genes.
In contrast, the GIL16–GIL01 comparison systematically shows the smallest absolute differences across nearly all topological metrics. These two networks are markedly more similar to each other, with lower mean and median differences and reduced variability. This pattern strongly suggests that the overall architecture of GIL01 and GIL16 networks is much more alike than either is to GBJ002’s.
The most pronounced divergences arise in metrics describing local connectivity, particularly triangles and clustering coefficient. For the comparisons involving GBJ002 network, the absolute differences in triangles reach extremely high maximum values, highlighting substantial changes in the formation of tightly interconnected neighborhoods. Similarly, clustering coefficient shows wide upper-tail dispersion, reflecting large shifts in local node cohesion within the network.
Path‑related attributes, including harmonic centrality and average path length, show moderate but consistent differences. Again, GBJ002 network diverges more strongly from the other two, while the GIL16–GIL01 comparison remains the most similar, reinforcing the broader pattern across metrics. Meanwhile, eigenvector centrality and PageRank tend to show smaller average differences, although the maximum values can still be high for a subset of topologically influential nodes. This suggests that, although global influence rankings remain mostly preserved, some key nodes behave differently across networks.
Taken together, the results indicate that GBJ002 represents the most structurally distinct network, showing substantial changes across connectivity, centrality and clustering properties. By contrast, GIL01 and GIL16 are the most similar networks pair, with consistently smaller absolute differences across metrics. Although the number of genes varies across attributes, the overall trends remain robust: the topological organization of GBJ002 departs most strongly from the other networks, particularly in aspects related to local structure and node-level importance.


Code
datatable(na_diff_pos$summary_by_pair_attribute, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c(
  "Pair", "Attribute", "Genes", "Mean absolute difference", "Median absolute difference", "SD absolute difference", "MAD absolute difference", "95th percentile absolute difference", "Maximum absolute difference"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("mean_abs", "median_abs", "sd_abs", "mad_abs", "p95_abs", "max_abs"), digits = 6)








Most variable genes
In the interactive table below, different sums are shown to reflect how the attributes specified in the ranking_attributes argument vary across networks. The Pair networks sum column represents the sum of normalized differences for each gene across the selected attributes for a given pair of networks. This value captures both the magnitude and direction of change between those two networks. The Sum regarding columns extend this concept by aggregating the pairwise sums across multiple network comparisons. Specifically, for a given network, only those pairs in which that network participates are considered. As a result, these columns highlight genes whose attributes consistently differ when comparing one network against the others. For example, the Sum regarding GBJ002 column includes only those network pairs that involve GBJ002. Therefore, it emphasizes genes whose attributes show strong deviations between networks containing GBJ002 and those that do not. This makes it particularly useful for identifying genes that exhibit pronounced differences in the presence versus absence of a tectivirus, which is the specific contrast captured by this column.
Taking the above into account, the most variable genes regarding GBJ002 are:

The first three whose sums are highest are de novo transcript 59 (GBJ002-DNT_059), NADH-quinone oxidoreductase subunit M (GBJ002_02486) and hypothetical protein (GBJ002_05065).
The first three whose sums are lowest are glycosyltransferase 2-like domain-containing protein (GBJ002_02433), flagellar motor switch protein FliN-like C-terminal domain-containing protein (GBJ002_04432) and RND efflux pump membrane fusion protein barrel-sandwich domain-containing protein (GBJ002_01782).

If we focus on the differences between GIL01 and GIL16 networks, the most variable genes are:

The first three whose sums are highest are UPF0145 protein BCE_5284 (GBJ002_02376), proton/sodium-glutamate symport protein (GBJ002_02412) and major facilitator superfamily profile domain-containing protein (GBJ002_02656).
The first three whose sums are lowest are winged helix DNA-binding domain protein (GBJ002_00399), alkyl hydroperoxide reductase subunit F (GBJ002_03138) and replicative DNA helicase (GBJ002_02711).



Code
datatable(na_diff_pos$sum_summary, rownames = FALSE,
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Pair", "Pair networks sum", "Sum regarding GBJ002", "Sum regarding GIL01", "Sum regarding GIL16"),
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "Scroller"), options = list(
            autoWidth = TRUE,
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
          formatRound(columns = c("sum_pair", "sum_regarding_gbj002", "sum_regarding_gil01", "sum_regarding_gil16"), digits = 3)

title: Networks comparisons
text:
Negative networks

Distributions
In the distributions of the per‑node attributes for the negative networks, all Friedman tests are significant. Kendall’s W shows moderate agreement for degree, strength, coreness and harmonic centrality and weak agreement for betweenness and eigenvector centrality. For PageRank and average path length, the Friedman tests are significant, but Kendall’s W indicates that concordance across networks is essentially absent, meaning that the statistical signal arises from the large sample size rather than from meaningful differences. These results suggest that degree, coreness and harmonic centrality capture structural features of the negative networks that are consistently recovered across methods, indicating that they may reflect stable and biologically meaningful patterns of negative gene coexpression.


Code
datatable(distributions_neg,
          colnames = c(NULL, "Friedman p value", "Kendall's W", "GBJ002 vs GIL01", "GBJ002 vs GIL16", "GIL01 vs GIL16"),
          rownames = c("Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Harmonic centrality",
                       "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank", "Average path length (L)")) %>%
          formatSignif(columns = c("Friedman_p_value", "GBJ002_vs_GIL01",
                                   "GBJ002_vs_GIL16", "GIL01_vs_GIL16"), digits = 3) %>%
          formatRound(columns = "Kendalls_W", digits = 3) %>%
          formatStyle(columns = c("Friedman_p_value", "GBJ002_vs_GIL01",
                                  "GBJ002_vs_GIL16", "GIL01_vs_GIL16"),
                      backgroundColor = styleInterval(0.05, c("lightyellow", NA))) %>%
          formatStyle(columns = "Kendalls_W",
                      backgroundColor = styleInterval(c(0.1, 0.3, 0.5), c("white", "lightblue", "lightsalmon", "salmon")))








Differences by gene, attribute and networks pair
In the interactive table below, we can see, for each gene and its associated attributes in the positive networks, the difference between the attribute value in the first network compared to the second. Although it allows for a detailed view of the changes, it is recommended to view the relative changes in the table below, as this provides a clearer picture of the changes across all attributes. In contrast, this table is highly recommended for viewing the most variable genes as their attributes values increase or decrease from one network to another, so it is suggested to return to this table after viewing the rank_summary one, as it contains more details.
In this table, the normalized z-score difference corresponds to the difference for each gene and attribute between networks. The use of this metric is explained in the same section for positive networks.


Code
datatable(na_diff_neg$diffs_long, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c(
  "Gene ID", "Pair", "Attribute", "Value on net 2", "Value on net 1", "Difference", "Normalized z-score difference"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("net_2", "net_1", "diff", "z_norm_diff"), digits = 3)








Differences by gene and networks pair according to attribute
In the interactive table below, we can see, for each gene, the difference between the values ​​of the respective attributes in the first positive network compared to those in the second. In some cases, missing values ​​appear because one or more values ​​associated with the attribute were missing from one or more of the networks.


Code
datatable(na_diff_neg$diffs_wide, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c(
  "Gene ID", "Pair", "Degree", "Strength", "Betweenness", "Coreness", "Harmonic centrality",
  "Eigenvector centrality", "PageRank", "Average path length (L)"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("Degree_diff", "Strength_diff", "Betweenness_diff", "Harmonic_centrality_diff", "Eigenvector_centrality_diff", "PageRank_diff", "Average_path_length_diff"), digits = 3)








Magnitude of differences summary
Across the three pairwise negative networks comparisons, the results reveal clear and consistent structural differences. It is important to note that not all descriptive statistics are computed over the same genes, which means that comparisons between attributes must account for this variability.
Overall, the network pairs involving GBJ002 (GIL01–GBJ002 and GIL16–GBJ002) exhibit the largest absolute differences across most topological attributes. This trend is especially pronounced for measures linked to connectivity and node importance, such as degree, strength and coreness. In these GBJ002‑related comparisons, both the central tendency (mean and median absolute differences) and the measures of dispersion (SD, MAD, 95th percentile and maximum) are consistently higher. These results indicate substantial structural rewiring in the GBJ002 network relative to GIL01’s and GIL16’s, affecting a wide range of genes.
In contrast, the GIL16–GIL01 comparison systematically shows the smallest absolute differences across nearly all topological metrics. These two networks are markedly more similar to each other, with lower mean and median differences and reduced variability. This pattern strongly suggests that the overall architecture of GIL01 and GIL16 networks is much more alike than either is to GBJ002’s.
The most pronounced divergences arise in metrics describing local and node‑level connectivity, particularly degree, strength and coreness, where the GBJ002 comparisons reach notably higher upper‑tail values. These elevated differences reflect substantial shifts in local structural cohesion and node roles within the GBJ002 network.
Path‑related attributes, including harmonic centrality and average path length, show moderate but consistent differences. Again, the GBJ002 network diverges more strongly from the other two, while the GIL16–GIL01 comparison remains the most similar, reinforcing the broader pattern across metrics. Meanwhile, eigenvector centrality and PageRank tend to show smaller average differences, although the maximum values can still be high for a subset of topologically influential nodes. This suggests that, although global influence rankings remain mostly preserved, some key nodes behave differently across networks.
Taken together, the results indicate that GBJ002 represents the most structurally distinct network, showing substantial changes across connectivity, centrality and path‑related properties. By contrast, GIL01 and GIL16 are the most similar networks pair, with consistently smaller absolute differences across metrics. Although the number of genes varies across attributes, the overall trends remain robust: the topological organization of GBJ002 departs most strongly from the other networks, particularly in aspects related to local structure and node‑level importance.


Code
datatable(na_diff_neg$summary_by_pair_attribute, rownames = FALSE, colnames = c(
  "Pair", "Attribute", "Genes", "Mean absolute difference", "Median absolute difference", "SD absolute difference", "MAD absolute difference", "95th percentile absolute difference", "Maximum absolute difference"),
  filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "FixedColumns", "Scroller"), options = list(
    autoWidth = TRUE,
    dom = "Brftip",
    buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
    fixedColumns = list(leftColumns = 2),
    scrollX = TRUE,
    scrollY = 400,
    scroller = TRUE,
    deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
  formatSignif(columns = c("mean_abs", "median_abs", "sd_abs", "mad_abs", "p95_abs", "max_abs"), digits = 3)








Most variable genes
In the interactive table below, different sums are shown to reflect how the attributes specified in the ranking_attributes argument vary across networks. The Pair networks sum column represents the sum of normalized differences for each gene across the selected attributes for a given pair of networks. This value captures both the magnitude and direction of change between those two networks. The Sum regarding columns extend this concept by aggregating the pairwise sums across multiple network comparisons. Specifically, for a given network, only those pairs in which that network participates are considered. As a result, these columns highlight genes whose attributes consistently differ when comparing one network against the others. For example, the Sum regarding GBJ002 column includes only those network pairs that involve GBJ002. Therefore, it emphasizes genes whose attributes show strong deviations between networks containing GBJ002 and those that do not. This makes it particularly useful for identifying genes that exhibit pronounced differences in the presence versus absence of a tectivirus, which is the specific contrast captured by this column.
Taking the above into account, the most variable genes regarding GBJ002 are:

The first three whose sums are highest are thioredoxin (GBJ002_02552), DUF445 domain-containing protein (GBJ002_04503) and ATP-dependent RNA helicase DbpA (GBJ002_02667).
The first three whose sums are lowest are BaiN-like insert domain-containing protein (GBJ002_01919), class II fumarate hydratase (GBJ002_04486) and phosphate butyryltransferase (GBJ002_01384).

If we focus on the differences between GIL01 and GIL16 networks, the most variable genes are:

The first three whose sums are highest are N-acetyltransferase domain-containing protein (GBJ002_00265), phosphoglycolate phosphatase (GBJ002_00174) and resolvase domain protein (GBJ002_02684).
The first three whose sums are lowest are CobW C-terminal domain-containing protein (GBJ002_04721), GNAT family N-acetyltransferase (GBJ002_04783) and HTH merR-type domain-containing protein (GBJ002_04383).



Code
datatable(na_diff_neg$sum_summary, rownames = FALSE,
          colnames = c("Gene ID", "Pair", "Pair networks sum", "Sum regarding GBJ002", "Sum regarding GIL01", "Sum regarding GIL16"),
          filter = "top", extensions = c("Buttons", "Scroller"), options = list(
            autoWidth = TRUE,
            dom = "Brftip",
            buttons = c("copy", "csv", "excel"),
            scrollX = TRUE,
            scrollY = 400,
            scroller = TRUE,
            deferRender = TRUE)) %>%
          formatRound(columns = c("sum_pair", "sum_regarding_gbj002", "sum_regarding_gil01", "sum_regarding_gil16"), digits = 3)
